text:
They heard your rant “It seems Victoria's public transport payment system is finally ready to join the 21st century, after years of frustrated residents demanding that we ditch those pesky Myki cards for good. It's just been revealed that from next year, we'll be able to tap on a train, bus or tram with a bank card – catching up to many other major cities around the world. It's about bloody time. 

When the news of a trial kicking off for the new payment system came out late last year, we allowed ourselves to dream. But now, it seems our dreams really are coming true, as the Victorian government has announced that bank card tap ons will be rolled out across Victoria's public transport from the beginning of 2026. 


The new contactless payment system will allow passengers to tap on with a credit or debit card, as well as with Apple Pay on phones and smart watches. First, 20,000 new card readers will be installed across our state's train network, including metro and V/Line trains. Then, it will be extended to trams and buses. Initially, it'll only be useable for full fare passengers. 

It was first announced that the system would be getting an overhaul back in May 2023, as part of a plan to eventually phase out physical Myki cards. This was finally put into practice with a trial beginning in November 2024.”

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
@heapsyeah he’s not the sole reason why the trams were retained he obviously played a massive part in the trams retention but there were other factors such as the lateness in which electrification of the tram network was completed, public and engrained political opposition, the introduction, effectiveness and proliferation of the W-Class tram and the Olympics.

Sydney were beginning to remove their admittedly antiquated tram network at pretty much the same time that electrification of the network in Melbourne was only just being completed as well as proliferation of the W-Class tram at around the same from 1920’s onwards, so when the opposition to trams came at that time in the 30’s 40’s the obvious clap back was why spend all this money electrifying the network and buying all these new trams to only rip it all out like only a couple of years later?

The W-Class which is now an icon also played a massive part, it was in its time cutting edge technology it was fast and super reliable due to its rugged design that literally had them still fully operating into the 1990’s and you still see them in the CBD to this very day, in comparison with the rickety trams Sydney were removing when the W-Class was being introduced they might as have been spacecraft it can’t be understated enough just how important the super dependable W-Class was and why it became an iconic piece of Melbourne’s cityscape it was a masterpiece.

And then Melbourne won the right to host the Olympics in 1956 and because the trams existed they were seen as vital at that time for being able to host the Olympics so they were retained at least into the 60’s by which time the public opposition to removing them had grown even stronger.

That didn’t stop the Libs from drastically underfunding the trams for the next 2 decades in an effort to undermine them that lasted until John Cain took power in the 1980’s and we got the Z-Class and the rest is history you probably know…

Awesome stuff Ray, very interesting seeing an outside perspective on our city, and a good reminder to Melburnians that we're actually really lucky with what we have here!
A couple of things: 
-Regarding road vehicle behaviour around the in-street tram stops, it's not just normalised in local culture, it's also taught specifically in driver training. Doesn't help when people drive in from other states, but anyone who's learnt to drive in Victoria *should* know the rules around driving with trams. Sadly accidents do still happen.
-The new metro tunnel stations at Town Hall/State Library arguably have different names to Flinders St/Melbourne Central to avoid confusion over their slight separation, but a more likley reason is so the government can say 'we're building 5 new stations!'
Anyway, keep up the good work. ❤

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: Yeah everything in an Australian city is a suburb. Inner-city Melbourne counts as a "suburb" of Melbourne. It's influenced in part by the British tradition of cities being divided into tiny sub-cities, in part by the Australian postal system where most suburbs get their own four-digit postcodes (although sometimes adjacent suburbs have to share postcodes), and in part by the Australian Bureau of Statistics' Statistical Area divisions -- what we call a "suburb", they call an "SA2 division".

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: Once upon a time, a suburb (outside the urban core) had a commecial district centred around a railway station. Residental buildings spread outward on a grid plan. Once you were about 10 minutes walk away, a new commercial zone centred on a "general store" or "milk bar" was created. The residential housing was intermixed with parks and reserves. This "patchwork" zoning surprisingly made a 10 minute city work with low-density, single family homes on large (1/4 acre) blocks.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: @neilworms2 Yes, but technically the CBD itself is also a suburb, it just doesn't get its own name. If you were referring to it in conversation you could call it "The CBD" or "The suburb of Melbourne" and most locals would understand either option. Exactly which option would be best depends on which city you're in -- in Melbourne they almost always use "CBD" because the CBD has a strict geographic boundary, but in Sydney the CBD is longer, narrower and hillier, and it's divided into multiple suburbs, so pointing to individual suburbs within the CBD is more common.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
Yup, the term suburb has different meanings in Australia and the US.

And "cities" and local government in Australia are also quite different to the US. In the US, cities run a lot of services (like police and schools) and have much more autonomy. In Australia, the state governments control all of this and have a lot more authority and operate most services.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
Love the outsider view. But the thing is with Melbourne is once you get beyond the inner ring of suburbs, mostly beyond the tram network, it does become very different and very car-centric again. Mostly built from the 50s right up to present day. The buses are a weak point and don’t have the same level of usability as the trams, although the train network is a great backbone if you’re near enough to a station, and want to go somewhere along that line only. Some still have at least shops that are within a walkable distance in a pleasant environment, but go to the outer suburbs and it starts getting very American.

Ultimately it’s like the other mainland capitals. Excellent, vibrant inner city areas but massively sprawling without any will to develop our other smaller cities (Geelong, Bendigo, Ballarat in Victoria for example), into great, liveable but smaller footprint cities. Something like 67% of Australians live in the capitals and they’re growing at twice the rate of the rest of the country. Melbourne would be the perfect size if it ended where the trams did.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
In the north of Melbourne, Bell St in Preston is really the exact arterial road between where the inner city ends (south of it) and the suburbia urban hellscape begins (north of it).

In the south of Melbourne, I would argue it is (ironically named) North Road the divide is. North of it is the nice inner city, south of it is where the urban hellscape WITH STROADS begins. Unfortunately Citynerd didn’t see enough of Melbourne 😢 - it is a clusterfck of great and terrible urban design. He would be having nightmares for days if he went to Hoppers Crossing!

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
I will say one huge advantage that Aussie capital cities have over major American cities though are the expansive usually electrified commuter train networks that operate at 20-30 min frequencies, the only American cities that have this sort of thing deep into sprawlly (outer)* suburbs are NYC and the Bay Area (with BART and Caltrain to an extent).  This is really underrated IMO, its nice to be able to at least have some transit that's accessible to far flung areas - most US cities don't have that and even in the (outer) suburbs of some of our best cities like Chicago get trains once every 1-2 hours outside of rush hour on unelectrified lines for the most part.

*Aussie term for what Americans would just call suburbs - car oriented places contrasted with inner suburbs which are streetcar suburbs / inner city neighborhoods in the US.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
@CityNerd there's definitely a sharp divide as you approach 10km or more out from the city. As good as the tram network is, it fades in the "middle" burbs onwards, and it does a lot of heavy lifting to mask the issues in our heavily radial train network. Melbourne can be very walkable, but only under someconditions.

Trams are good, only if you are in the inner suburbs (and not in the CBD because then they are slower than walking)
Trains are good, only if you strictly travel to and from CBD for your commute
Buses are good, only if you live near the few high-frequency routes that get dedicated lanes or avoid peak hour traffic routes
Cycling is good, only if you live near the suburbs that have improving bike infrastructure (or dont fear death)
Walking is good, only if you live in the CBD, inner suburbs, or a local commerical hotspot

That being said, there are lots of improvement slowly being made, and it is possible to live a car free life even in the outter suburbs, provided you have access to at least a couple of the options above. I have friends who's able to commute to the city from 40km in the same amount of time as another who lives 10km away thanks to a good Vline and bus combo

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
What amazes me is that us older folk want to go out into the bush or to rural towns and spend our money supporting them. Nothing like a feed from a good country pub and a stroll around the town and browse through their little second hand stores.
Every second week me and the missis head out of the city bush walking and often spend time in places like Castlemaine, Benalla, Bright, Mansfeld, Port Fairy and Torquay. Thats just this year, been doing it for a few years now. I have lived in the urban sprawl for 60 years, and need to get away on mini holidays every now and then.
Melbourne has got too big, too quick. Need to build the infrastructure out in the burbs more before we take in more immigrants, it is way out of control.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: Great point and I think that's the difference between Docklands and other inner suburbs of Melbourne even. Docklands was designed to be a dense, mixed use, urbanist environment but because it was pretty much built from scratch with very little retained or reused, it lacks all the culture, character and charm of the older inner suburbs. And while things like trams running in mixed traffic are a flaw that somewhere like Docklands wouldn't repeat in the modern era, it actually adds to the charm and culture of the old suburbs. Cars stopping behind dinging trams in the middle of busy streets is just "so Melbourne" it creates that unique sense of place.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: @halleyorion For my country it can be a synonym of 'faculty' while a 'school' in a university usually refers to a smaller faculty, each overseeing multiple departments that each take care of  university majors similar to each other e.g. the college of social sciences overseeing the departments of language studies, geography, economics, history, communications, political science, European, Japanese studies. However also, dormitories that offer modules/courses exclusive to them (that're recognized as general knowledge modules that all majors in my university require) are called residential colleges. Other dormitories include halls, which don't have these exclusive modules but more co-curricular activities instead e.g. inter-hall sports competitions, & residences, which have less of such activities. We also have the term "junior college" to refer to schools teaching students at our equivalent of 11 & 12th grade, or what many other countries call "high school" instead

​@jacobmtaylor When I moved to Australia from the US, this is exactly what my partner told me to say. He cringes when he hears people with rhotic accents skip their natural Rs.

The main error that Americans make when saying "Melbourne" is that they tend to pronounce it as if it's written with American spelling rather than British/Australian spelling. In American English, the inclusion of the 'u' indicates emphasis (e.g., we write "color" because last syllable is de-emphasized, but we write "velour" because the last syllable is emphasized), so the 'u' in Melbourne induces an instinct is to say "mel-BORN" instead of "MEL-burn".

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
On the trains:
The naming is really strange. The trains are mainline heavy rail trains, and the suburban lines share track with the intercity and interstate trains, so it's not a metro system, but the private operator is called "Metro".
The direction of travel in the loop changing in the middle of the day is a historic artefact. There used to be a huge railyard next to Flinders Street station, where the suburban trains were stabled. Prior to the loop existing, suburban trains would terminate at Flinders Street and get shunted to said yard. The loop was built with this in mind, so morning trains would run through the loop, getting to Flinders Street last to terminate there as always. In the afternoon, the trains would get out of the yard, start at Flinders Street, then run through the loop and then leave the CBD, which resulted in the direction change. That railyard is long gone, but some lines still reverse direction twice a day, with the slight benefit of distributing passengers more evenly across the CBD instead of having full trains regularly entering the extremely busy Flinders Street.
I learned this from the channel Taitset, who's a local Melbourne train nerd with some excellent videos on the whole system. Definitely worth watching if you like trains, even if you're from the other side of the world from Melbourne, like myself.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: @shrakaIMO the elevated rail lines were an absolute must with our fast growing city, as you said it certainly improved traffic, which had to happen as Melbourne swells with more traffic, but a major safety necessity as well. Having had trains cancelled because (in my experience), the death of 2 elderly ladies who got stuck on train tracks & a school girl getting killed at a level crossing, and others, it served a dual purpose for the traffic/pedestrian danger now and looking to the future when it will just be busier.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
@ You’re thinking anecdotally rather than statistically. Trains are 10 times safer than cars. Anything that induces more car demand or higher speeds increases injury, thus the elevated railway probably increased injuries overall. Adding regular trams to shuttle people to train stations and increasing train frequency / adding in a real metro would have actually reduced injuries - potential significantly. A lot of the roads could just be terminated rather than being crossings. Eventually elevation is a good idea, but it DEFINITELY should have been triple track capable, at least leaving room at the stations for a bypass.

If planned properly a new tram network / metro would induce extra economic activity and reduce demand on roads - so the freeway widening project could have been reduced in scope (or just dumped entirely). Those savings / increased activity could result then be spent on a slightly delayed elevated rail project. As it stands the state is broke.

Paris is getting about 30km of new metro for the cost of the elevation project. Even if we halved that efficiency we could get about 40km of brand new state of the art metro line if we dumped the freeway projects, and have some left over for new tram lines.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: @shraka Maybe, but the severe train delays caused by accidents like those stick in passengers' minds, and many of them decide to stop taking the train and start driving instead, since cars can just route around the accidents. While multi-hour train delays are not frequent (we were affected by just two of them during the year we lived in Reservoir), they were HUGELY inconvenient for thousands of train passengers, and the effects that echoed for days while the accident site was repaired. We personally decided to stop using trains for time-sensitive travel (namely daily work commutes) after that. Thankfully we had other options (buses and bicycling) for our particular commutes, but not everyone does.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: @ Given how packed trains are I don't think it's causing much mode shift. You're again thinking anecdotally rather than statistically. A new metro service with interconnecting tram lines would allow those in dense enough areas to have multiple rail PT options to route around any accidents, while also saving lives. An actual metro would be far more frequent too, making transfers easier. The larger more complete network would also make for less rail user disruption once the elevation project did go ahead.

When you said you couldn't determine if Melbourne is a coastal or river city, it's both and then some more. What I love about Melbourne is depending on where you go, the vibe can change considerably. Palm trees and beaches, busy city life and laneways, rolling hills in wine country, windy roads through rain forests, or country town feels, there is a huge diversity to chose from just within the limits of greater Melbourne.
Another oddity about Melbourne is you can be 30, 40, 50km out and you'll still see it's impressive skyline towering over the surrounding area. It has the most high rises in the southern hemisphere. Yet  there are only a few areas were you really experience these high rises towering over you once you are actually walking within the city. Even in the CBD, the most active areas like around Swanston Street and China Town still tend to feel human scale. Very different to Sydney for example which scale wise feels much grander and taller, similar to what you would experience walking around Manhattan.

text: @psychic_beth Exactly! Adopting different accents for specific words (including place names!) is just not how phonetics works. There's an underlying form of the word that is rendered differently in different accents, and when you start to mess with it arbitrarily, it's uncomfortable to listen to. Nor is it realistic to do this consistently for every accent or dialect in every place: imagine you had to read a list of cities, adopting in turn the accents of Melbourne, Liverpool, New York and Birmingham. That could be a fun party trick, but it's not a normal way to talk

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
I really appreciate an outsider looking at our network, it's a change of perspective considering people in Melbourne tend to think our network is not stunning. Although Melbournian's tend to think PT as a method of transport for everyone, a contrast to how people in the USA think PT is for poor people (from what I know, & excluding NYC). The different names for Town Hall & State Library is because emergency services wanted seperate names, so it's more clear what's what when responding to an emergency.

The City Loop or 'the Loop' changes directions of travel due to when it was built there was large rail yards in Jolimont (just north of the MCG), so in the morning trains would enter the loop finishing at Flinders Street before heading to the yards, doing the opposite in the PM. 2 line groups, the Hurstbridge & Mernda lines (Clifton Hill group), & the Pakenham & Cranbourne lines (Dandenong / Caulfield group) don't change. The Clifton Hill group is due to a poor junction layout at Jolimont, & the Caulfield group is because the HCMTs are longer & required signals to be moved but was only done for 1 direction. For more a proper City Loop explanation, Taitset made a video about it which explains it very well.

Another thing which is aimed to solve what you pointed out, the highly radial design of the Metro network is the 'SRL', which is controversial but is needed. It's going to fully automated (SRL East & SRL North which will be 1 continuous line), & 1 of its purposes to create more TOD around its stations that aren't already built up (so it'll create TOD or put the 'T' in TOD).

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
As a Melburnian, progress here is slow. Everything the city's best known for is a pedestrianised space, our laneways are the heart of the city, and yet there's been no strong move to add to this for decades. Even the roads closed by the construction of the Metro Tunnel are due to be reopened to traffic. It feels appropriate that we're slipping down the liveability rankings because other cities are actually doing something! I look forward to the changes to zoning around centres (in response to housing crisis), and the slow decades to come of building trains, but this isn't real urbanism.

The suburbs often aren't centred on pedestrian spaces, they're focused on a road, and everything good about them exists in spite of that. The best of our wonderful cafes are on side streets, a minor throw from the frequent traffic. Everything's inundated by traffic noise or closed in by parked cars. There's no great movements to improve this, everything's fought against by businesses who consider a loss to parking and cars a threat to their livelihoods. The trams are slow because of the cars, the bikes lanes are often disparate, the standards used to improve roads are inconsistent leading to varied traffic behaviour. Our outer suburbs are worse, they're often sprawling car-centric hell-holes with big box stores, maze-like suburbia with no transport. These places drive up the traffic everywhere else, and we're still adding more lanes to ring roads to "fix" it.

I don't know if you just have to live here for a decade to see it, but it's quite depressing to see it stagnate and sometimes even worsen through entrenched car-centric attitudes. It's sad to see large street upgrades result in another road, just with with some greenery and a bike lane. It's not enough, and yet Melbourne is still a nice place to live in spite of it.

---
Also: you can now add a myki to your phone, and it doesn't require you to buy a physical one. Also, you can top up a myki with your phone without going to a machine.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: I used to live in Melbourne a couple of years ago, and never had a car there so go a good idea of how the transit works. In the CBD, it's usually faster to walk than take the tram lol, they are so crowded and slow. I honestly found the trams annoying most of the time. Often late and extremely slow. Trains could be okay, but they were very unreliable on the two lines I used the most. Would very often just not run on weekends, which was so frustrating. My local bus also only ran one direction in the morning and the other in the evening, and not at all on weekends - and given I was half an hour walk from the tram or train, this was rough. Having lived in North America, I totally get that Melbourne has good transit compared to some other cities, but I honestly found it a pain.  It's also expensive - $5 to go one stops is ridiculous. Melbourne is EXTREMELY sprawly and believe me there are plenty of stroads and unworkable areas further out. I do miss the food in Melbourne though! Sorry America, but the food here sucks in comparison.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: The city loop reverses direction at midday because when it was built there was a depot in south East Melbourne in Richmond. So the loop was organised to send trains into the depot in the morning and bring them out in the evening. The Richmond depot has long since closed but trains still follow this tradition as a lot of the signalling was set up for it. Meanwhile here in Adelaide we used to have a 11 mile  one way freeway which used to switch directions at midday.  Demand for it grew so after 13 years of being one direction it became a regular two way free way.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
@bigoldyoungin There's stuff all space at Burnley compared to the old Jolimont yards, and it only serves one group of lines (the aptly named "Burnley group").  Trains being stabled off-peak now run out all over the network, rather than all being stored centrally ready for the evening peak.

The loss of the Jolimont yards is also why there's only limited footy specials these days.  Now there's only basically running lines in the CBD, so there's very limited space to stage the trains near the MCG ready to go.

text:
This is so cool to see! I ran into you very briefly and got a photo (I nerded out so hard to my girlfriend later lol) when you were out for dinner in Moonee Ponds and I've been so keen to see what you had to say about Melbourne since that. So cool to see you talk about MoPo in good depth as well. I moved into a share house like a 5 min walk from Puckle St, and at first it felt to suburban for me after being near the CBD, but I realized that this is the sort of suburban that is actually awesome to live in. I still have a private house (as private as 4 people living together can be) with plenty of space, a garage, a little yard, but everything I need in my day to day life is a 10-15 in walk away, and my journey to the CBD for work and uni only takes about 30-40 mins. I live in the suburbs, but get all the benefits of a walkable neighborhood and a city, without being forced to drive everywhere which makes it so much cheaper and easier to live.

I wish I had been able to actually chat to you when you came in, but unfortunately it was a busy night at the wine bar. I hope you had an amazing trip in Melbourne and enjoyed your stay in Moonee Ponds!

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: In Australian English “suburb” literally just means “neighbourhood” it does not in any way convey a level of urbanity like it does in America, we even have areas that are known as “the inner city suburbs” which would be oxymoronic in American English. I’m not sure how this definition first developed, but it was formalised  by governmental surveying and mapping in the nineties. Interestingly the idea of living “out in the suburbs” is still a concept Australians understand and use.  We also pretty much ignore local government boundaries, the entire Melbourne and Sydney metro areas are just Melbourne and Sydney.  The NSW State Government also arbitrarily redraws the local government boundaries weirdly often. I don’t even know which local government areas or cities my previous Sydney Metro Area addresses are in these days. Addresses use the suburb name not the local government area, and local government is responsible for a lot less than in the US. All of this why when I lived in Oakland all my Australian friends and family thought I lived in San Francisco, from an Australian perspective the whole Bay Area is San Francisco. 🤷‍♀️

This was my 2nd time in Australia (2nd visit to Melbourne) and like you, my main focus in Melbourne was to spend much of my time in the neighbourhoods/suburbs. They are fantastic from both a pedestrian and transit perspective. Overall the city just feels easy to live in if you don't have a vehicle. I didn't feel limited to what I could see and do just because I was walking and taking transit everywhere. The motorists also seem much better behaved than anywhere I've been in the USA (though I did notice Melbourne motorists seemed more prone to honking than in Sydney). I love Melbourne and can't wait for my next return, most likely in early 2028. Looking forward to your Sydney video!

It's great to see your views on Melbourne. I moved in to Fitzroy from Glenroy about 2 years ago, and it's been great. Having started in Brunswick when I moved down here in 2007, and then living in North Melbourne, Essendon, and a whole bunch of suburbs, the rule is generally tram for inner city or trips from suburb to suburb, or trains from outer suburbs to the city.

Fitzroy is trams, or walking, which I think is something that both goes hand in hand with the public transport options to boost liveability. I never find there's anywhere that's off limits for walking, really. I have no problem walking from my home to somewhere like Southern Cross.

Also, Easey's is great. Last time I was there we got the end booth and my son ate nothing because "TRAIN!" 😄

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
Hell YES!!!

I’ve harped for years and years and then some that urbanist YouTubers particularly those of the North American variety should absolutely 100% focus on Melbourne and also Australia.

But Melbourne, Melbourne in particular is exactly the standard that North American cities should be aiming for it’s not perfect, it doesn’t have bike lanes out the asshole (although it does indeed have a steadily growing network that punches above its weight) it has some really awful car infrastructure but it still has awesomeness mostly due to it’s public transport on a level that most cities (including imo Amsterdam) fail to replicate.

It’s obvious to me as someone that lives here and watches you, NJB and RM Transit and others that it has a ton of upside that you all could be talking about relentlessly, even (and yes this is going to be controversial) in the much derided outer suburbs.

Mostly though because of exactly what you said in the opening stages, Melbourne is not an ancient 500-1000 year old city it’s exactly along the timeline of most US cities only with better foresight.

Yes Melbourne got lucky but it doesn’t mean that US cities couldn’t or shouldn’t strive to build places like those strewn across Melbourne, most US cities have the bones to make it happen but lack the willpower.

Either way I like your video Ray it’s excellent especially because you were only here for 2 weeks, you could 100% spend a lifetime exploring and talking about it.

There’s also something to be said about the nature of the transit to cafe relationship in regard to Melbourne which also has the most cafes per square kilometre of any place on earth…

I’m glad you enjoyed your time here mate I’m sure if your only bugbear is the antiquated fare system (which will be changing… next year) I think we’re doing well.

Just spewing I didn’t know otherwise I might’ve come along to RMIT but I’m sure you’ll probably find your way back here at some point.

And also completely juxtaposed to ‘Give Yourself The Green Light’ is ‘Citizen Tram’… Melbourne propaganda for the tram!

Also I’ll post a video about AFL and how it works in another comment for you…

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
Our Tram system in Melbourne works for us, they carry workers to work and home, sporting fans to events, you get on trams in Melbourne for theatres, museums, exhibitions, music arenas, football etc.  They are the icon of our city and we don't want them to change, thanks very much.  There are also lots of motor cars in Melbourne CBD, you don't see parking on the street often because there are underground parking areas, multi car parks and other street parking areas, so yes, there are a lot of us who do drive in the cbd. Also the tram culture you mentioned for drivers.  How we stop and start for trams is part of our drivers license learning booklet, you have to pass the test to drive and that's part of the test.  It's illegal to pass trams at stop!  So if you have good road driving rules that are followed you won't have issues with how to follow trams.
Plastic cards are the best way to pay for travel in Australia, keep your Apple to yourself please. Australia is not the US and it doesn't want to be!

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: The reason why some lines in the city loop still reverse half way during the day is because pre 1994, Jolimont yard was one of the major stabling points for suburban trains. The idea was that trains in peak hour would come in from the suburbs, go around the loop and then shunt to Jolimont yards. Then in the evening peek the trains would go the opposite way around the loop to take commuters back out into the suburbs. although the Mernda and Hurstbridge line were made to go clockwise around the loop in 2008, due to a conflicting junction at Jolimont. And the Cranbourne/Pakenham line was made anti-clockwise when the signaling was adjusted for the new HCMT trains to run in the city loop.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
12:08 - The North American definition of "City" vs "Suburb" just doesn't work at all in Australia, because Australia doesn't really have municipal govts and a distinction between city and not-city. Services that would typically be run by the cities in the US are run by State Govts in Australia for the most part (like transit), and local councils are glorified planning committees subservient to State Governments, there's no legal distinction between an urban council and a rural council although they might call themselves "City" or "Shire" if they want.

Australians use the word "Suburb" to mean "formally delineated part of a city", like wards or districts, even the Melbourne CBD is a suburb by Australian useage. Unlike Neighbourhoods Australian Suburbs are formally defined and a part of a location's address used by the postal service.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
Hey, Ray, Melbournian here! Hope you enjoyed Melbs. 

Did you get to see the Nightingale residencies off Sydney road (Brunswick)?

Some of the best examples of transport-oriented ‘community’ development and they’re all built by a nonprofit organisation. 

It’s the only source of hope for young people like me to get into mixed-use transport-oriented areas, as they build small apartments and townhouses, often with no parking included, and no price padding for the developers profit. 

Most young people in Melbourne nowadays are pushed out of such areas to the desolate outer suburbs that are composed of single-family homes, big-box stores and an absence of viable public transport. 

We have a massive issue of suburban sprawl and, as someone that lives near the airport, it sucks.

I have hope that we can make the most liveable parts of Melbourne more accessible to the less privileged through the zoning reforms that the gov is doing.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: On a map of Melbourne stick a pin in the CBD and draw a circle 10km/6 miles in radius. Roughly speaking, that's old, walkable pre-car Melbourne. Almost everything CN talked about lies within this circle. It's nice, to be sure, but it's very different from the outer suburbs built in the post-war era. I used to live in St Kilda - now I live in Clyde. I'd love to show CN the other Melbourne, the miles and miles and MILES of soulless, car-dependant urban design disasters that we've insisted on building for the past 50 years. It's such a shame he stopped at Carnegie, which is practically an inner suburb these days. He should have kept going south-east and visited the real Melbourne (real: where most Melburnians live).

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
Great video post, made me homesick. Melbourne originally had a large extensive cable car network, basically they replaced these vehicles with electric trams in the early/mid 20th century. It’s a shame they didn’t keep/preserve one of the cable tram lines. It would’ve been iconic if they had. Also, some of the old tram storage depots are fantastic pieces of architecture, Malvern, Kew are some good examples.
Looking forward to your upcoming Sydney post. Great work and your insights on urban environment are spot on.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: At the top end of Swanston St, near the City Baths and just before the road angles right to head up to Unimelb, is a white building, isolated on a small island between three roads. That's the old cable station. As far as I know it's the only remnant of the cable car system.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: @TheRealPotoroo They are called cable car winding stations, the engine rooms that powered the cables that the cable cars would connect/grip to. Basically steam driven power houses. They are still there. One exists on the corner of Nicholson & Gertrude streets (Fitzroy) and one on Johnston street (Collingwood) which is now a supermarket I believe. There are photos from the time, some had tree ferns growing inside, so Victorian era. There used to be this awesome pavilion that was located behind the Victorian museum/library that displayed a Melbourne cable car, it included displays and windows in to how the cable teams worked, things like the grip car, how the mechanism worked etc. It was demolished when they did the library infill expansion in the 1990s. Melbourne’s cable cars had two carriages, one was the grip car that was open seating and had the driver operating it from a position in the centre, the other was an enclosed passenger car. There are YouTube videos showing this off. Just type in Melbourne cable cars. Marvellous Melbourne!

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
Looking at the footage, i think the trams are intimidating to cars.  The trams are HUGE, and BOXY, and _"Wait my car can't eat this thing"._  This makes cars scared to be anywhere near Trams, and by extension: scared to be on the road.  As most of us here can probably understand:  This is actually... good.

I've been in Melbourne a few times, it was a while ago but i do remember this - when a Tram slams on the brakes & screeches to a halt, then lays on it's horn (bell?), coz your CAR is blocking it's way (the tram can't go around it's on rails) - that car is fucking terrified.  These are not roads controlled by cars, and the Trams (love em) make that audibly & visually known as often as it should be ✊.

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text: Melbourne trams are built like absolute tanks aside from the W and C classes (W's are old and made of wood while C's were bought from Alstom for cheap in the early 2000s and are pretty crap). the older Comeng-built trams are basically just solid steel and the huge bumpers on the front absorb most of the energy in a collision, and usually they emerge from accidents with hardly a dent. the newer E class trams instead have more aesthetically pleasing bumper designs that crumple to absorb energy, but they're by far the heaviest trams in service on the network, no car can deal severe damage to them. even in tram-bus collisions the tram still wins every time

videoTitle: What Is Livability? A Field Report from Melbourne
text:
I am an Indonesian who briefly lived in Melbourne, for about one year and a half.

For the first six months, I lived in Blackburn South, a sprawling suburb. The nearest tram stop was twenty minutes walk away from the place I lived. While there was a bus stop, the schedule was unreliable. Not to mention, it was a very unwalkable place.

Then, I lived in Box Hill. While the walkability was also rather poor compared to the CBD area, it was very transit-oriented. Not only my apartment was just less than five minutes walk away from the nearest bus and tram stop, it was also about ten minutes walk to a central station that covered both buses AND trains.

Oh and I still miss the trams. It was the first and only time in my life that I lived in a place with relatively reliable mass transit.

Melbourne buses aren't as reliable. While I have heard complains about the trains, I don't have anything to say about them, as I only used for one day. Literally for one day during my entire stay.

text: The Yarra River is like that for good reason. The whole south side of the river used to be alluvial swamp and the plant are no longer there to filter out the silt and nutrients. So the places you like there have emerged from draining all those marshes and billabongs.  I think I'd prefer things as they were...

videoTitle: Melbourne, Australia 🇦🇺 - The Most Livable City in the World? | Victoria, Australia Travel Guide
text:
The soil in the Yarra River was contaminated many years ago. Upstream from the City is an area that was the "Rag Trade". Cotton dying in particular where dyes and industrial waste was discharged into the river. So to fx it now requires dredging the bottom. Governments have avoided that.
Further up the Yarra River is pure clear water.
We have a joke that says : The Yarra runs upside down, the bottom is on the top.    🇦🇺

videoTitle: Melbourne, Australia 🇦🇺 - The Most Livable City in the World? | Victoria, Australia Travel Guide
text: @justshowupshow We haven't had any significant bushfires since the last big one in 2019-2020. However, an accident happened up in the northern country region about 90 km from Melbourne, and it's now burning for a couple of days now. We usually get them when we have prolonged dry and windy days, but usually the temp drops down for 10C-20C in minutes when we reach our hottest peaks. You should be fine if you move within the inner suburbs of Melbourne.

text: This videos was very helpful! I'm moving to Melbourne in May and was looking for information since I don't know much about the city. In your experience there, do you think it's easy to find a place to rent? I don't want to spend too much money in hostels while waiting for a place to live.

videoTitle: 9 Things You SHOULD KNOW Before Moving to Melbourne | Moving to Australia
text: Glad to hear the videos are helpful :) Regarding your question, during the past years, it is true that competition for rental properties and prices, especially in inner city suburbs has gone up significantly (what you could get for 400pw, is now 450/500 pw for example). Having said that, renting is not impossible as many people and the media portray (most of my friends are still able to find rentals, even in inner city suburbs - so it's not all doom and gloom - even back in 2019 you'd compete against other 20 people for an apartment in Fitzroy or East Melbourne (I've been there), but if you go to other areas where supply is relatively higher than demand, it shouldn't be that bad). As long as you are flexible in terms of areas to live in (perhaps a bit further from the CBD or beaches)/and property type, and you are able to show payslips or a bank account demonstrating you've got income to cover rent for the period of your rental contract and have good references, you should be fine. Also, realistically, you should allocate a minimum of 3-4 weeks until you find a place to live in (between researching and inspections you can expect that amount of time as a min.). I have a few videos on how to rent property in Australia, you may want to check that out :) https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EbSOxeNcRKs

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? | Pros and cons of living in Melbourne
text:
Jamie I'm also from Perth (north of the river, not too far from the ocean) and have also lived in Melbourne for years and years. I miss Perth/WA beaches too! 😭 I was a "water baby" growing up, thankfully my lifestyle isn't beach-dependent as an adult but I'd still like to have the option of being able to go to a great suburban beach without leaving the city.

I actually like the changeability of Melbourne's weather, for the most part, because I know that if there's a scorching 40 degree day there's a good chance of the heat breaking tomorrow. A reprieve. Whereas in Perth summers it's just way too damn hot, over and over.

And I empathise with those memories of musicians skipping Perth!

I'll add a pro: Because there are lots of people in Melbourne who are from elsewhere, I think it's easier to strike new friendships, people on the whole are less insular and less limited to their existing/longstanding friend groups.
And a con: How long the winters are. I don't mind the cold so much, but winter seems to start earlier and end much later here than in Perth.

text: On the beaches - Greater Melbourne offers awesome beaches and I've been snorkelling here for almost 2 years now, i.e., Ricketts Point, Half Moon Bay, the beaches of the Mornington Peninsula, etc. These beaches are only 15 minutes to an hour's drive from the CBD and sure, the waters are cold, especially in the autumn and winter months, but I love the sea life and diversity here. I guess it also depends on people's preferences, i.e., tanning, picnicking, amenities, and the like. But if that's the case, most of the beaches from the CBD to the east and to the west (extending outside Greater Melbourne) have tons of these to offer as well.

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? | Pros and cons of living in Melbourne
text:
I definitely agree with Patricia & Adrian on their points (I’ve lived in Melb my whole life). If you’re into a more fast paced city, a BIG night life, more diversity in people, food, cultures, entertainment + you’re okay with unstable weather (carry a small umbrella lol) and you’re good at managing your time (because you often have to factor 1-2 hours of commuting into your plans 🥲 painful sometimes) then you’ll vibe with Melb really well. The beaches in Melb are definitely pathetic😅 but you can have day trips or even a night away/weekend away when you explore beaches outside of Melbourne (there are some stunning areas around Victoria). Road trips are soooo fun on a summer day!

I’ve visited Perth a handful of times & have family that live there to provide insight. It’s muccch more laidback & traffic (driving) isn’t as long or intense from experience. During my visits, I realised 40 minutes drives were considered ‘long’ by my family in Perth because everywhere is fairly close/ quick to drive to. The weather seems pretty damn perfect and predictable, the near by beaches were nice. Only downside for me was shops closing so early (I think 5pm? Someone pls correct me) and the lack of night life (because I’m too use to the Melb night life). Otherwise, it was very calming and relaxing ☺️

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? | Pros and cons of living in Melbourne
text:
Public transport in Melb is pretty good especially closer to the inner city areas & it’s much more ideal than driving or there is the option of using city ‘rented’ bikes or electric scooters (which are fun to use in summer) and you can download an app to find where they are scattered and rent it out (which you have to pay for). 

Haven’t tried PT in Perth. Maybe someone else could provide an update on that ☺️

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? Our HONEST thoughts
text: Having lived in Melbourne all of my 70+ years and having family overseas, I have travelled a bit, and think Melbourne feels quite  European in that we have preservation orders on a fair bit of the early architecture. It also has a similar weather experience to the UK and Europe minus the snow. Summer feels like a good day in Spain or Italy and so on with good days in the other Seasons to parts of Europe. We just shrug our shoulders when it rains and say “give it an hour or two and the sun will be shining”. The multi culture of Melbourne has been a thing since the Gold Rush days of the 1800’s and as we never had a penal colony in Victoria, we tend not to worry about where a person came from or why they came here. Accepting and welcoming is probably a hallmark of being a Melbournian but your always going to find the odd outliers unfortunately. My recommendation to newbies to Melbourne is to download the PTV App to plan your public transport journeys.

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? Our HONEST thoughts
text:
I've lived in Melbourne (outer eastern suburbs) all my life, although I have traveled to a few other cities within Australia and in China. All the cities in Australia, and that's including the regional cities like Geelong and Newcastle have way more positives than negatives. I like to visit the city from time to time and one thing you see in Melbourne these days that was really not much of an issue 20 years ago, is the amount of people sleeping rough. The government addressed the problem during the Covid lockdowns, so why not now? 

Regardless of a persons circumstance, a progressive society should at least see that everyone within it has access to shelter, food, water, and health services. It's a black eye for Melbourne, and indeed any city within Australia, that the problem is not even being addressed at a political level.

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? Our HONEST thoughts
text: @Ozvideo1959 homelessness is an epidemic for a while. Like you I live in the outer east. I haven’t been to the city in some years but even then people including quite a few females were neatly lined up in sleeping bags on the bridge crossing the Yarra from Clarendon Street to Spencer Street probably because there was overhead shelter (possibly installed for tram users) and the police were patrolling the area on foot. Locally, last year I just couldn’t bring myself to “dob in” a homeless person who pitched a tent under cover of a rotunda in our local park as everyone deserves shelter in Winter and it is a shame on the Government that the homeless that want basic housing can’t get it.

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? Our HONEST thoughts
text:
Melbourne's weather can be  'four seasons in one day'. (The band, 'Crowded House,' even wrote a song about it.) So, the trick is to dress in layers to put on or take off. Also, we've been experiencing soggy summers over the last three years due to three negative weather patterns converging- that of the Indian Ocean and Pacific Ocean dipoles and Antarctic upflow. When you experience a real heatwave of three days plus of extreme heat of up to 45 degrees Celsius you will appreciate the cool change in summer. And yes, always your plans should include changeable weather. The weather forecast report should always be noted!
     Melbourne is a huge city. The geographical area of it is almost 10,000 square kilometres. So it will take a while to get to nature areas. Melbourne is known as 'the garden city', with many large gardens and parks.  You did not mention our train transport system which does extend to outer areas in Victoria. There are about 18 beaches around Port Phillip Bay, which Melbourne surrounds, and most are accessible by tram or train.  For most other ocean beaches you will need a car. Melbourne's tram system is the most extensive in the world.
     The reason why we have so many beautiful, old buildings is because Melbourne was the richest city in the world in the latter 1850s due to the huge amounts of gold found in the Golden Triangle in the north-west country-side of Victoria. Also, importantly,  the building unions conscientiously refused to continue to pull them down to make way for newer buildings by developers. Newer skyscrapers are interesting to view having more creative features. The H&M was once the General Post Office, from which distances are measured.
     Your vlog is somewhat limited but eventually you will explore more of Melbourne and Victoria and have more to report. Glad to have you here. ENJOY!

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? Our HONEST thoughts

Hey, thanks so much for watching!!!

videoTitle: Should you live in Melbourne? Our HONEST thoughts
text:
London has a ton of issues now. A lot of them invisible in affluent areas, but as anyone born and raised there or moved there from other developed countries would know, it really is a different story in the standard middle class and working class areas in outer London where you’d be living in. There’s a somewhat depressed and unsafe feel to the entire city. Melbourne has a more positive, down to earth, can-do vibe that London simply doesn’t have. 

London pre-pandemic was a redeemable place at worst, excellent place at it best. But now it’s a “conveyer belt city” and ordinary parts outside the affluent inner city tailored for tourists and the ultra rich feel forgotten/abandoned. Very little sense of community. Hard to make real friends there. There’s a clear segregative aspect to society there where people don’t get to know each other outside of work and you find severely siloed communities based on language groups, religion and culture that rarely communicate with each other. 

Not to mention all the social and economic issues like high crime,  rent on average taking up a half to two thirds of one’s salary, and energy bills being so high that many Londoners no longer can hear their homes during the worst months of winter. 

If you want to get a true feel for life in London before making your decision, listen to LBC - it’s a London based talk radio station very popular with the masses from all backgrounds. Listening to it for a few minutes you’d very quickly realize how London is not a very nice place to live. 

So as of 2025, I’d recommend NOT moving to London. And I was born and bred there. Melbourne is a far better and livable choice.

Also @9:28, is Melbourne is Australia's cultural capital. And it's also the 2nd largest city in the country overall? Then that's rather interesting to me. I guess that Australia anyways? Shares that cultural similarity to Canada. As Toronto is the financial capital, but then Montréal it's 2nd largest city overall? Is generally viewed as the cultural capital of Canada. (Or at least the most unique & entertaining of North America's largest cities). Toronto anyways? Is often viewed as highly Americanized & certainly ALOT more Americanized than Montréal is. Which is a city for the most part? Could only really exist in Canada or any other bilingual country anyways. Where BOTH English & French? Would be official languages. Thus, making Montréal seem/ appear alot more culturally interesting relative to Toronto. (Because of this dichotomy). And a city overall? (That being Montréal). Where the US really has no answer too. Also @11:36, Melbourne's winters aren't that harsh, maybe for an Australian, but if you're moving to Australia from either Canada or a Scandinavian country? Then Melbourne's winters? Are a breeze. Also @13:24, yes you're right, both Sydney & Melbourne? Are amazing cities really, & certainly in their own ways. But for me personally? They'd be too big. If I was to ever live in Australia? Then more than likely I would choose either, Gold Coast, Sunshine Coast, Canberra, Adelaide, or Perth.

text:
Sydney is far more beautiful, but Melbourne has a far more impressive skyline. 

Sydney is miles ahead of you have money or are thoroughly comfortable with multiculturalism and learning a new language to live in your suburban comfortably. Melbourne is far better if you enjoy being a white, artistic, music-loving, emo Australian who doesn’t mind a touch of multiculturalism and overt public displays of racism, and tin-foil hat hatred of the government, as well 10 minutes at a level crossing, no beaches, roads that have no bends like the Nullabor, and your cycle hitting a tram track groove and launching you into a car waiting to do a hook turn. 

I personally particularly love the thousand dollars in fines I racked up as a Sydneysider because I drove Sydney style at no more than 5kmph above the speed limit (because I was looking up and concentrating on the road, not the speedo) and the absence of warnings meant that every f*cking bridge or underpass I drove under hit me with another f*cking fine. 

I love Melbourne but let’s be serious. There’s as much to whinge about there than there is in Sydney (don’t get me started).

videoTitle: 🇦🇺 What Makes MELBOURNE Better Than SYDNEY In AUSTRALIA? 🤔
text:
Melbourne = Seattle/Boston, Sydney = San Fransisco/Los Angeles. I feel like it’s too hard to compare them both, it’s all up to the vibe.

In a negative light:

Sydney is all about the rush and crush to pay the bills and hope for a day at the beach or by a river for a picnic with the family once a month.

Melbourne is about trying to find your purpose in life, finding your social circle and where you’re going to end up constantly.

videoTitle: 🇦🇺 What Makes MELBOURNE Better Than SYDNEY In AUSTRALIA? 🤔
text: It's about actually living here, for years, raising a family, getting too know the details, rather than just visiting as a temporary resident or work visa holder.  Sydney is prettier with its monuments, Harbour and fancy beach live locations, and just great for shorter stays.  Melbourne is where you want to live, to experience the lifestyle, friendlier community (multi cultures and not just the coffee), the sports, the gardens, and more. It's nice to have the Opera House and other pretty sites that you visit occasionally, but as someone actually living here, other daily matters are more important.

videoTitle: 🇦🇺 What Makes MELBOURNE Better Than SYDNEY In AUSTRALIA? 🤔
text:
Every way in which Melbourne beats Sydney (arts/entertainment, sports), Sydney comes 2nd.
The ways in which Sydney beats Melbourne (beaches, beauty, architecture) Melbourne isn't even 3rd. 
People who are militant about their arts and culture will prefer Melbourne. 
People who are happy with a slightly inferior (but still great) arts and culture scene packaged in a beautful city with beaches, prefer Sydney.

Food scenes are equal and anyone who says otherwise is talking nonsense.

videoTitle: 🇦🇺 What Makes MELBOURNE Better Than SYDNEY In AUSTRALIA? 🤔
text:
This is such a weird question - it's been buggin me all day. As a Melbournian, I never ask this question - Sydney just doesn't have anything I'm interested in, and always seems more suited to tourists and rich people than Melbourne.  The real question is "Why do I love Melbourne?" and I have just too many answers to give for this one... but the two most obvious ones: I love the coffee culture (and nothing beats a well made Magic) and the food culture (which is forever evolving). If beaches and scenery are more your thing, then good on ya! But it's Melbs for me, forever :)
P.S. Sydneysiders always seem to be asking this question, though... it sounds a little desperate and insecure to me.

text:
@peterfromgw4615
Okay, so where in Melbourne can I catch a ferry to an island which is UNESCO heritage listed with convict buildings as well as the entire 20th century history of naval history, 15 minutes from the CBD (Cockatoo Island) then go to the largest Chinatown in the Southern Hemisphere followed by a short walk past the only genuine Chinese Gardens in the country to the world’s third largest fish markets? All in the morning, before heading to a concert at the most visited opera house in the world? 

And that’s not even scratching the surface with the UNESCO listed Blue Mountains next door, the second oldest national park on the planet to the south (Google “Wattamolla”) and the Hawkesbury to the north. 

Then there’s the cheapest trip to Asia when heading to Cabramatta or Eastwood., hitting little India in Harris Park just next to the oldest remaining building in the country (Elizabeth Farm), or the thousands of years of aboriginal rock art everywhere that makes people say that Sydney is the “largest outdoor gallery in the world”..

Tuck your head in champ. Melbourne is spectacular and so very different, but don’t ever f*ckin diminish Sydney in a childish attempt to compare such different things.

videoTitle: 🇦🇺 What Makes MELBOURNE Better Than SYDNEY In AUSTRALIA? 🤔
text:
Specifically?
So I've lived in Melbourne all my life and visited Sydney extensively since young (with some gap years) because I have relatives in Sydney, the reason that a lot of people prefer Melbourne over Sydney is the people and the reason people prefer Sydney over Melbourne is the weather and views. I've met a few ex-Sydney people who are living in Melbourne as a permanent shift and they pretty much told me they hate Sydney. I think the predominant thought was that Sydney people are pretentious, snobby and similar. You know, out to be seen, whereas they said Melbourne was more relaxed and more welcoming.
For me personally, every now and then I would bump into people who were kind of arseholes. Not overly so, but just enough that it can be noticeable. 
I've always enjoyed Sydney as a short stay city. I don't know if I'd want to live there.

videoTitle: 🇦🇺 What Makes MELBOURNE Better Than SYDNEY In AUSTRALIA? 🤔
text:
I think a lot of people who claim all sorts of things in Melbourne are special have not spent much time in Sydney.

It is likely that Sydney has much the same as Melbourne if people bothered to look. 

The big advantage for Melbourne is its sports events which the Victorian state governments have pursued aggressively and effectively.

They bring people to Melbourne who may not have been interested otherwise.

Sydney tends to rely on its physical attractions, rather than events since most events happen in the same arc of the harbour.  

While there are plenty of other attractions, the accessibility and variety of beach and coastal attractions draws the majority of visitors.

Since the weather in Sydney is generally of the ’goldlocks’ variety:  NOT too hot, too cold, too windy, too sultry, too wet, too dry, but just right, the outdoor lifestyle is preferred over the indoor culture of cafes, museums, and shows.

text: Melbourne weather is only ever variable during late spring and summer, where temp differences can really be felt. Outside of those months, it's stable and consistently cool-cold. Melbourne is also largely colder than Sydney even though they only vary by 5 degrees across median temperatures because of dew point. Melbourne has lower dew point than Sydney which means Melbourne is not humid compared to Sydney. Dew point affects actual perceived "feels like" temperature. 15 degrees in Sydney may feel like 15/17 where as 15 degrees in Melbourne, can feel like below 10 degrees.

videoTitle: LIVING IN SYDNEY VS MELBOURNE AUSTRALIA: Lifestyle Comparison
text:
We live in Sydney's Upper North Shore, for which there is no real Melbourne equivalent.  There is a fair bit of consolidation going on, but it is still an area of comfortable family homes on reasonable blocks of land - in imperial the blocks are a half acre and in metric I think that works out to about 200ha.  It is hilly so even walking a suburb away gives plenty of exercise for an aging couple. Melbourne is much flatter. There is a good train service to the city - in our case 35 minutes and in the days I was working that took a bit over a half hour.  We'd get much the same in Melbourne despite major differences in the style of passenger rolling stock.   Plenty of suburbs have day-to-day household shopping (ours has restrictive covenants which ban any commercial activity) and a couple of larger centres for larger purchases such as furniture and appliances.    Good public and private schools.   We'd find similar suburbs in Melbourne.  The climate is cooler than closer to the harbour or coast, and on a couple of occasions we've had very light snow (it had all melted by the following morning).  The Melbourne climate would be similar.

We don't really notices differences in the art scene.  We have had subscriptions for many years for theatre and opera and we'd get much the same performances were we in Melbourne.  On the sporting scene, there is no Melbourne equivalent to the surf beaches in Sydney suburbs.  We don't have easy access to Rules football but in Melbourne there's very little of either rugby code.

videoTitle: LIVING IN SYDNEY VS MELBOURNE AUSTRALIA: Lifestyle Comparison
text:
I grew up in Sydney, and have lived in Melbourne for the last 20 years. There is one simple huge difference which no one seems to talk about. It is summed up in one word: NATURE ! If you are interested in Australian nature, Sydney has a "great outdoors"; Melbourne does not. Sydney is surrounded by beautiful national parks, right on its doorstep, offering superb bushwalking and wildlife-watching, especially the fantastic native flowers and birdlife. To reach any decent national park from Melbourne, you would need to drive at least 3 hours from the city centre. All this beautiful nature overflows into the Sydney suburbs, which are much lusher due to Sydney's higher rainfall. Melbourne's dry suburbs and parks offer mostly European trees and a few introduced pest birds.

Sydney has 3 major estuarine systems (Pittwater, Sydney Harbour, Port Hacking) giving it a stunning scenic coastline, and offering fantastic fishing, snorkelling, scuba diving, and boating. Of course the real surf beaches add to the nature experience of Sydney. None of this can be found in Melbourne.

So, after you have had your fill of all the Melbourne restaurants & cafes, and all the totally man-made entertainment that Melbourne is so proud of, where do you go on a sunny day for a little nature soul food? Where can you escape to in less than an hour where unspoilt natural beauty is free? Nowhere.

Melbourne lovers are generally pure city people who couldn’t care less about nature. Well, I couldn’t care less about sport or coffee. I feel totally trapped in Melbourne.

text: Bellasie As someone who had been living in Melbourne for more than 4 years before having moved to the west coast. I find Melbourne to be one of the most diverse and inclusive cities in this world. Yeah, some Melbournians do complain about “annoying” tourists like the rest of the world does, but it is mostly a lighthearted joke in the real life. Hardly any xenophobia or racism. It has become probably one of the least racists city in the world. Trust me, the majority of Melbournians are in fact very welcoming towards outsiders. I assume this comment was only an reaction towards Sydney’s status as the Australian city that’s is more well known to international visitors. “Melbourne vs   Sydney” rivalry has been a tale as old as the time since the founding of these two settlements. At the eve of the federation of Australian colonies, they literally had to select a new site (now ACT) between these two cities to build a new capital (Cabrera) as a compromise. Unfortunately, this rivalry has taken a more toxic form on online forums. This thread feels more like a childish rant saying, “if you tourists like Sydney, our shinning big brother, that much, you should just all go there and leave us alone. We don’t need you here anyway.” It is almost like the bitter feeling of the second child of a family when he or she has always being overshadowed by its more favoured sibling. However, this child is in fact warm and kind. Come to visit Melbourne, and you will know how amazing this city is (apart from its horrible weather lol). I am saying this as some who was originally an outsider yet gradually become more and more attached to this city. This thread is really not what Melbourne is about.

videoTitle: Sydney and Melbourne Compared
text:
Not a bad assessment. As an Aussie living in Sydney, I must say that both cities are generally in the 'great' part of the spectrum compared to other capitals around the world. And I am reasonably well-traveled. My son just came back from Melbourne last week, first-time solo - you see things differently when not traveling with your parents. His observations were pretty much what I feel too. 
Melbourne generally is a better, easier city to live in - there's always something to do and the key population hubs are well connected because of the trams to the CBD. Melbournians are generally a little bit more culturally diverse, as in being genuine 'culture vultures', and that's always a joy to experience too - so it's fair to say that Melburnians celebrate 'thinking different' more than Sydneysiders. Part of that reason is that the city isn't really as geographically interesting as Sydney so the residents have less distraction in that area, making it up in intellectual and artistic pursuits - the geographic landscape is more a blank canvas compared to Sydney. My son's biggest negative comment was that Melbourne doesn't have a proper beach. He's a surfer, so makes sense. Having said that the Melbourne beaches closest to the CBD just aren't that compelling or dramatic - nice, but boring.
On the other hand, Sydney is ridiculously blessed with some of the best geographies a city could wish for! If you are fortunate to live within close proximity to the Sydney CBD, and I am, every morning you wake up, it's another day in an urban paradise. The city is surrounded by a string of beaches that people literally travel for 1000s of miles to see and experience, and they're 2nd rate compared to the beaches 30 minutes up or down the road from the CBD. Sydney is a serious competitor when it comes to the food department too. It's just that you generally need to drive to these great food precincts  Vs walking or catching a tram in Melbourne. Sydney is a much better 'lifestyle' city, hugging the entire Sydney Harbour and Middle Harbour and reaching inland along the Parramatta River - the entire city coastline is pretty much open as a public thoroughfare and you could ride your bike, canoe, sail, jog or fish around the city for days and never see the same spectacular scenery twice - all in beautiful, warm weather, mostly. Consequently, Sydneysiders are amongst the fittest, best-looking and healthiest group of people on the planet - due to accessing their homegrown healthy, lifestyle pursuits. Plus, the historical aspect and general pride in the city is also pretty much second to none. I always think of tourists when asked the question, which city is best. The consensus is Melbourne is just lovely, I had such a good time - I could live there. In Sydney, the tourist comments are narrowed down to, OMG! My god, this place is unbelievably beautiful - there's nothing like this anywhere else. Monaco and Copacabana all rolled up into one. Everything is so expensive!
I always use the adage, if Melbourne and Sydney were women, Sydney would be the hot, brash, saucy modelesque girl wearing not much, because only she could get away with it and Melbourne would be the mysterious, intimate, elegant lady dressed in a flattering, more modest, but still sexy attire. Both are great cities. Both are worth the visit. You make up your own mind. Either way, you will not be disappointed.

text: We have to deal with blizzards here, and when I mean blizzards, I mean BLIZZARDS. I’m talking more than 50, 70 and almost 100 centimetres of snow. That’s almost bigger than a mailbox or a street sign here. And we once had the ARMY come in cause all of this snow led to a state of emergency here. We even had to get the US army’s help. God that was friggin mayhem. Schools closed, pile up on a Montreal highway, articulated buses in Ottawa crashing and getting stuck, the subway in Toronto getting constantly delayed because nobody wants to be on the surface, the power going out in most of the cities, freezing rain, the sewage system breaking, bridges getting closed fearing someone might yeet off the guardrail, the snow plow crew in 3 of the cities being suspended because oh goodness, the snow was big, heavy and tall, holy, it was simply Arctic that day. And that was almost 10 years ago! God, y’all are lucky not to live in a Siberia temperature like this. And tomorrow as I’m commenting this, we’re expected to get at least 10 cm of snow and our first freezing rain storm of the year. Oh well. But I gotta admit, wildfires and bushfires are far more worse.

videoTitle: Sydney and Melbourne Compared
text:
"See Both!" was the right answer.

Sydney for both natural and man made sights and the brilliant Asian food.

Melbourne for the Art, Entertainment and European food.

Both for the coffee, all food generally and Art scenes.

Despite what was said (which was pretty accurate) both are very easy to get around with by public transport. And easy to explore on foot. Melbourne is more concentrated than Sydney and the Trams really are a huge plus for getting from A to B. Sydney is building its own tram network which will transform the heart of Sydney, but they are making a real hash of it at the moment. Hopefully the mess will be cleaned up by 2020.

If you want to see a lot in a short time in Sydney, catch the ferries - all of them. The Harbour is fantastic from the water. And you can cover a vast area not just in Sydney but out of Sydney on the huge train network. I particularly recommend the trains north to Newcastle which skirt the edges of Broken Bay and Gosford Inlets. The early trips are magic in the morning mist across the water. Take the southern train to Woollongong through the Royal National Park and down the Illawara Escarpment overlooking the Pacific Ocean. Take the western train up and over the Blue Mountains for spectacular vistas down the deep valleys. All are very cheap and reasonably quick considering the distances travelled.

Melbourne take the trams everywhere. The tramlines are lined with fantastic shopping and places to eat, so just hop on hop off. There is even a series of Restaurant Trams with fantastic food served to your table whilst you circle the City. It really is buzzing with creativity: art, fashion, food, design and entertainment. One of my favourite cities in the world.

Canberra got a brief mention but is well worth a visit. For a small City it is stuffed with things to see and also has a great food scene. It won the World Barista Championships twice in a row, and brews, distills, cures and grows a dazzling variety of great food and drink. Even though it is a city you will see just about every Australian native flora and fauna just walking around its streets and parks.

This is only the southern corner of Australia though. Plenty more elsewhere. Its a big place.

Australia is a continent with a vast interior, and many climate zones, not  an island archipelago,  The nation is all the better for having put the capital where it is.

videoTitle: Sydney and Melbourne Compared
text:
Great video, you really picked up the differences between the cities.  A few corrections: I have never heard of “The Archway”; the photo suggests this is actually the laneways of Melbourne.  There are actually nine AFL teams in Melbourne.  In addition to Melbourne and North Melbourne, they are Hawthorn (the best, of course), Collingwood, Richmond, Essendon, Carlton, St Kilda and Western Bulldogs.  Australian football was invented in Melbourne in 1858.  Sydney were never good enough to play, so we had to give them the South Melbourne Swans who became the Sydney Swans.  There are also many other Rugby League teams in Sydney other than Sydney and South Sydney but who cares?  The Dandenong is actually Mt Dandenong or the Dandenongs.  

The rivalry between the cities actually goes way back.  The colony of New South Wales (aka Sydney) sent an expedition to Port Phillip (on which Melbourne is sited) in 1803.  They picked a spot with no fresh water and so the expedition failed.  Ever since, Sydneysiders have considered the area to be worthless.  A bunch of free settlers from Northern Tasmania decided that they wanted to settle the area of Port Phillip and asked permission from the New South Wales governor and were promptly refused permission.  They had to sail to London to get permission,
which was granted.  Once the settlement that would become Melbourne (by the way, it was also called Bearbrass at one time) had begun, the settlers decided to have a land auction.  Suddenly, Sydney became interested and said “Don’t worry about that; we’ll sort it all out” and took the profits.  Hence the long term knowledge by Melburnians that Sydneysiders are only interested in money (it’s true by the way; I have lived in both cities.  Melburnians are obsessed by sport and Sydneysiders by money).

Haha great video Mr. Beat.
I'm a proud Melburnian and would never move, but Sydney is definitely a beautiful place. As a muso, I think Melbourne has a way better live music/pub scene, with heaps more places for bands to play, but Sydney is honestly a prettier city aesthetically, even though Melbourne has it's charms too.

Yes the Young Brothers grew up there, but Bon Scott grew up in Fremantle, and Mark Evans and Phil Rudd are from Melbourne, AND the early years of the bands real existence were in Melbourne when they all lived in East St Kilda, so really, AC/DC are an Australian band with not one city that can truly claim them.
For anyone from overseas thinking of visiting, you don't have to pick one, just go to Melbourne AND Sydney and you'll love both for different reasons.

videoTitle: Sydney and Melbourne Compared
text: Pretty fair comparison -- though I was thinking all through the video what you finally came to at the end -- visit both. Only thing I'd say that Melbourne has more of than Sydney is places outside of town to visit -- Ballarat, Sovereign Hill, Bendigo, Phillip Island. But both are absolutely worthwhile. And then there's Brisbane, which gets you closer to the tropics and Great Barrier Reef. And Adelaide, which puts you in the center of wine country and close to opal country. Of course, having traveled all over Australia, I'm rather fond of the whole place. And Perth, especially in the spring, where they have the most extensive wildflower displays in the world. And Alice Springs. And Darwin. And Broome. And, and, and.. (First trip, I covered roughly 20,000 miles -- and even wrote a book -- Waltzing Australia. And I've been back several times. Too much to see in one trip. But I'd definitely tell folks to aim to see more than Sydney and Melbourne, because ultimately, they're big cities, and heading north into the tropics or inland, to the "back of beyond," you get to see what makes Australia different from everywhere else.)
