text:
In May of 2008, my mom and I visited Melbourne and Sydney. Both were fun, beautiful and extremely clean cities. Melbourne reminded me a lot of San Francisco and Seattle, where I live. Sydney reminds me of a cleaner Los Angeles w/better air. I also lived in Los Angeles before moving to Seattle. 

When it comes down to which city I prefer, it's Melbourne. It feels like a more livable city, and it was easy to find things to do such as going to a play, visiting the museum, shopping, etc. I also spent a day in Victoria's wine country, which was also nice. Sydney however is a more impressive city. The Harbour Bridge, The Opera House, Circular Quay and The City Centre are just as impressive in person. They have a very cool aquarium in The Harbour, more tourist attractions and some pretty cool places to shop as well.

As far as music is concerned, I can't name a single music act from Melbourne off the top of my head. But AC/DC and INXS do immediately come to mind when I think of Sydney. INXS also happens to be my favorite band and I've attended three of their concerts in Kansas City, where I grew-up! Finally, if there're any INXS fans visiting this page, Michael Hutchence's Memorial is also in Sydney. I of course paid it a visit!

videoTitle: Sydney and Melbourne Compared
text:
I traveled to Australia for nearly three weeks back in August, 2015.  (Didn't mind the fact it was winter.  I grew up in cold winters, and Aussie winters are mild - and I was doing volunteer work outside and would rather have a high of 20 vs 35C).  Entirely in NSW.  2/3 of my time was in Newcastle, where I volunteered for a conservation organization planting trees on old pastures at a nature preserve.  They housed us in a central location to the beach and to the downtown where we could walk to beach/park as well as pubs/cafes, library for wifi, etc. 


The other 6 -7 days were mostly exploring Sydney, and an excursion to the Blue Mountains/Katoomba for hiking/trekking.


As an American, while I would absolutely love to visit Melbourne if/when I get back to Oz, I think that if one is VISITING or living for a short time and want to have iconic Australian experiences, Sydney is the better option.  


Melbourne might have more hipsters livelier arts and indie music scene and that is great.  But the US has MANY cities with that.  Melbourne might have more music and art festival, as well as annual sporting events like the Melbourne cup, Footie, etc. but unless you follow those sports and are there at the time those are going on that's not likely to factor in strongly for a decision as to where to go.  


Sydney has a much more beautiful physical setting and nearby natural features for recreation and outdoor enjoyment.  Sydney has the harbour with sailboats going by, has multiple points and peninsulas, etc.  Melbourne has a small river flowing through a flat plain.  Beaches and surfing are a big part of Australia's image. And Sydney has way better and many more beaches.  Victoria has mountains like the Snowy mountains and the Grampians, as well as rugged beautiful coastal areas like the 12 apostles/great ocean road, but they are hours from Melbourne and if you have limited time, and want to experience Australias "bush" and National Parks, you can spend a whole day in the Blue Mountains, hiking and enjoying scenic views and get back to Sydney for dinner and drinks.  


If you like history, Sydney does have landmarks dating back to the original convict days like the Rocks, Barracks, etc.  Like he mentioned Melbourne wasn't founded until the Gold Rush later.  


Like Beat mentioned, Sydney has a great foreign born population, and I imagine more options for Asian cuisine.  I live in California, so have no shortage of Asian cuisine at all, but in Sydney I had tasty Malaysian food, and Thai food that was very different from what I experience in the US.  


You might call them tourist traps, but there is nothing like the Opera House and the Harbour bridge anywhere.  And Sydney has amazing cultural and historic landmarks similar to Melbourne such as Hyde Park, Royal Botanic gardens, Queen Victoria building, the University, etc. etc. 


No one is paying me to promote Sydney.  I just think it is the better option of you in Australia for a short time.  If you are living there for the long term, and are more of a city person who doesn't  prioritize getting out of the city for the day, then Melbourne might be a better option for some.

videoTitle: Sydney and Melbourne Compared
text:
Live in Melbourne (Mel-bun) - visited Sydney lots

Melbourne is more cohesive than Sydney. Very suburban. Sydney is very individualistic - full of mavericks - all about the money. Melbourne is all about what school you went to,

Sports is massive in Melbourne - Sydney not so. Crowds are way smaller in Sydney. A big divide is that Melbourne is all about the AFL (Aussie rules) which is where it was invented. Melbourne has most of the AFL teams. While Sydney has only 2 AFL teams and it really is all about rugby and rugby league. Basically Victoria, Western Australia and South Australia are AFL states, and Queensland and New South Wales are rugby states. In Melbourne there is a massive sports precinct literally within walking distance from the CBD, 100,000 capacity MCG, as well as a soccer rugby  ground and tennis where the Aus Open is (as well as a seperate 60,000 seater stadium just on the other side of the CBD - all within walking distance).

Most of the more prominent politicians come from Sydney. Most of the national cricket team is from Sydney. Sydney is more picturesque and is on the ocean with lots of real beaches. Melbourne is on a bay - it's 'beaches' are like visiting a pond - literally no waves at all. So Sydney definitely has the beach culture - Melbourne has none.

Only about 10% of Sydenysiders get to live the iconic Sydney beach / bay thing - they are the lucky (rich) ones - the other 90% live in a sprawling, fractured, dry wasteland. The.Melbourne experience is more consistent / accessible through out.

Parts of Sydney have a large middle eastern vibe / community thing going on. Melbourne has lots of Indians and Greeks - also a very tight Jewish community that live almost exclusively in two or three suburbs just south of the CBD.

For restaurants, cafes, shopping, a show or a major sports event - definitely visit Melbourne.

For iconic scenery and travelling on ferries and beaches - definitely visit Sydney.

The past few years though, I think prices for things are much the same and even out. Motels and petrol are definitely much cheaper in America though.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text: As someone who has now lived in Melbourne, originally from Canada, for 15 years, I’m still constantly amazed at the number of beautiful places to visit.  There are at least 15 little towns outside of Melbourne that are worth a trip on their own, countless natural places to enjoy (hundreds or thousands of them), so much culture, so much incredible food, don’t forget the hundreds of wineries, so much atmosphere to soak up, so may festivals, and so much life.  It’s an incredible city.  It does take time to find places, as Australians are 1) Unaware of how special all these places are, 2) Not culturally likely to shout and advertise these places.  Aussies are quietly proud of a beautiful country with lovely people, but it’s all normal to them.  Melbourne and Victoria are incredible places to visit and love and you could easily fill months with exploration and relaxation.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text:
I've lived in Melbourne since 1981 and the changeable weather is something that even Melburnians have problems understanding. The things is that Melbourne is on the southern coast of Australia, which means that to our northwest, we have the Outback, which is the place where DRY and HOT takes on completely new meaning, but the ocean to our southwest, is the Antarctic Ocean, so the wind direction only has to change through 90 degrees for the air temperature to change quite dramatically. I remember in the early 80's, one day in Summer, when the wind had been coming from the northwest and the temperature was 42 degrees Celsius (107.6 deg. Fahrenheit) in the afternoon, the wind changed to coming from the southeast and the temperature dropped to 23 degrees (73.4 deg. Fahrenheit) in less than half an hour, which makes sense of the Melbourne saying; "If you don't like the weather in Melbourne, just wait a moment." 
I live on the northeast borders of the urban sprawl (which is one of the largest in the world) so I can be in the countryside within less than 5 minutes drive from my home. I'm not sure if you managed to explore the Yarra Valley, which is right next to the outer suburb I live in, but it is one of the prettiest place in the country, bordering on the Victorian high country, which is a stunning place to visit. 
What still gets me about Melbourne, is that if you go up to a lookout in the Dandenong Ranges,, east of the city, when you look out over the suburbs, mostly what you'll see is trees. You can see a few rooftops of houses and some of the shopping centres and industrial buildings in nearby suburbs, but from up there, the city look mostly like a densely forested area, with a few signs of buildings here and there. 
The CBD is dotted with parks and that is the same for the suburbs, so that the whole place is VERY green and you're never far away from a park of some kind. Trees are everywhere and there's a very real aversion to cutting them down, which is very different to a lot of cities I've seen in other parts of the world. 
Thanks for the compliments in your video.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text:
It was wonderful to see my city of Melbourne through your eyes.  The best time to visit is in our Autumn, not only for beautiful mild days, but also the colours of the autumn leaves everywhere you look.  Sydney is beautiful but only for a visit of 5 days tops for me and then I want to come back to Melbourne where getting around is so much easier and everything is more accessible.  I like to say that Melbourne is a grand dame and Sydney is her brash younger sister.

Australia in general has been very aware of people with disabilities for decades, hence ramps to enter buildings, trams, trains and buses (if required), separate bathroom facilities, the sound of the traffic lights to notify when it's safe for pedestrians to cross the street and also the strip of raised dots on the streets in the city.

I find jet lag more brutal travelling from Europe to Australia than the other way around. Oh, and meat pies are usually eaten by hand with a dollop of tomato sauce on top or under the top layer of pastry.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text: Christmas Day, in Melbourne, we were running for our jumpers. My phone was saying it was 17 degrees, Celsius, but it felt like 11.2. And indeed it did The wind was freezing, especially when we were at some cricket nets at a park where there was no protection from the wind. But for the prior three years, we were in the pool! It has been a weird weather pattern this year. We had almost no rain in winter and the ground was bone dry and some plants died! but it started to rain on the first day of spring and has barely stopped. I try not to complain as we lived through a 10 year drought 20 years ago. But I’m sorry it caused issues for you. It would be very frustrating as a tourist.❤

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text: Melbourne has very changeable weather. Even in the height of summer we can have 40C (100+ Fahrenheit)and sunshine one day and dark sullen skies and 15C (59F) a few hours later. This is due to the constant passage of cold fronts across the southern ocean. The temperature increases with hot dry northerly winds before the front, with cold SW winds after the front. This spring was particularly annoying as a sudden stratospheric warming causes the frequency of fronts to increase so we didn't get more than a few days run of warm weather until December.

Do stay away for fire season. It can start from December, and continue into February. Avoiding that time also means you avoid the heatwaves. Other than that, there aren't any particularly good or bad times to be here. Winter minimums are just cold enough for me to pull my blanket over my head, autumn and spring are both lovely. text: As a Sydney sider I love Melbourne, but I have friends in Melbourne which makes it easier. The galleries, museums & theatre; the Library used to be the state library and museum, which is possibly why the library is so adapted to display. Almost all the big cities in Oz (and New Zealand) will claim some kind of higher coffee or food status. Melbourne can be a very indoor food city simply because of the changeable weather, but the biggest changes in all these cities came post WW2 with immigration from 'old' Europe. Sydney's original coffee centre was the inner city & up towards Kings Cross and the suburb of Leichhardt. Old Europe helped build Australia's largest project at the time the Snowy Mountains Scheme. With them came new food, modern ski area development and and explosion of cultural change, for the better, but you've not really seen Melbourne unless you've been to an AFL game at the Melbourne Cricket Ground. Happy travels.

text:
One thing about Melbourne that many people don't know is not what's in the city itself, it's what's outside the city and only a day trip or a day/night trip away. There are some fantastic regional towns to visit, there's the high country and of course there are the seaside towns. And Melbourne is sort of in the middle of all of them. It's a great springboard, as such. The other thing that Melbourne really has going for it is its live music scene. While it's not as good as it was, there are still plenty of live music venues with great quality
acts of just about any genre playing. "Coffee culture" is what we do at work. It's what we do on the weekends that makes Melbourne great.

text:
Hey guys, glad you enjoyed Melbourne. As a Melbourne born and breader I knew I loved my town even before backpacing overseas for 3 years in my 20's, unlike some mates who bought a one way ticket (common for us curious, isolated Aussies) but returned home after 6 months homesick.  I think they didn't appreciate what we had, whereas i knew it would always be waiting.

Thankyou for including the library (i like sitting in the reading room watching tourists gasp as they enter) however reckon you missed two, or should I say need to experience two activities NEXT time you visit; 1) AFL footy, born in Melbourne and our religion, non-denominational (unless you're a pies supporter) and surely more soul nourishing and good for humanity (unless you're a saints supporter, ironically).

And 2) which even Melbournians are unfortunately complacent about is our live music scene.  Seven days a week you can see anything from afro soul and latino funk to basement jazz and indie rock, and pretty much everything in between, such that, like our coffee, we don't mind inventing new types.

Anyway enjoyed your vid and gonna look up your tassie video, who, by the way will be the 19th team to enter the AFL in a couple of years. Just saying.

text: Melbournian here and I agree about Degraves St, I would never go there to eat because the food is all the same and it's just terrible. Fun fact about ACDC lane, it used to be called Corporation Lane until my son's dad opened a bar called Cherry Bar there in 1999. The bar became famous for being a hang out for lots of famous rock artists that would tour Australia (my ex mother-in-law used to make sausage rolls for them). Because of this, a group of people petitioned to have the name changed to ACDC lane. I'm so happy you took the time to learn about Indigenous culture and doing a coffee tour was a great idea because unles you go into the CBD a lot, it would be hard to know where to go. text: Years ago, there used to be a nasty saying: "Melbourne's a lady, Sydney's a whore". I don't hear it these days because the rivalry between the cities has settled down. There was a strong rivalry between the two cities that goes back to the early twentieth century when the six colonies of the continent became a united Commonwealth in January 1901. (It might go back even to colonial times.) Melbourne was the Australian capital initially. The leaders realised the competition between the cities meant a neutral place had to be found. Originally, Eden - a fishing village on the south coast of New South Wales - was considered - but they thought it might be vulnerable to seaborne invasion, and Canberra was selected instead. Canberra was designed on a huge sheep farm called "Yarralumla" by Walter Burley-Griffin - an American architect. He was assisted by his wife. Canberra drew inspiration from Washington DC in my view, (having been there), though much smaller. Many people are a little disparaging of Canberra, but I think it is well worth a visit. A week would be plenty - it is not much larger than Hobart. It is somewhat "contrived" but it nevertheless represents much that is Australian about Australia. There are vast open spaces, public gardens, sweeping drives, impressive government buildings etc. There are reasonably good bus services there, and the distances are relatively short, but if you are able, hire a car. There is little traffic, and the boulevards are wide. Like DC, it is full of circles, which can be slightly confusing sometimes. Glad you enjoyed your stay in Melbourne. I am looking forward to your impression of Hobart, which is one of my favourite places and a gem that too many people overlook.

text: I moved from Chicago about 40 years ago. First to Melbourne (2 yrs), then  Sydney (18 mo.) and  finally to Brisbane (20 yrs). Now I'm back in Melbourne. Had a few years in Scotland while in Brissie for my wife's post-doc. To me, Sydney is too American. Melbourne has a more European vibe (I've spent a lot of time in Europe - lived in the UK and briefly in Rome and my wife works part of the year in Copenhagen). Brisbane, at least when I lived there, was the most quintessentially Australian of the three. It was a great place to raise our kids. Culturally, Melbourne is tops. I've travelled all over the world and honestly could not imagine living in any  other country.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text:
@mahlonrhoades4509
All 3 cities are very different from each other and All are quintessentially Australian.  In fact, ALL Australian cities are very different, very Australian.   
You realise, in claiming something to be better, you have to make everything else worse, bad.   
I have family who live in 4 states all up & down the east coast including Hobart.  I have family that have also lived in Darwin, Adelaide & Perth.  I have family that live in rural areas as I once did.  I have family that lived in the UK & Canada.  One city, one state doesn’t work for me.  

Australians tend to travel between states frequently & effortlessly for holidays, family, work, change of lifestyle, retirement.  So much so, school holidays are staggered to allow more options for everyone & less traffic congestion.  We live in one or more place & holiday in others.  
Being different is what makes our experience in each place more meaningful, interesting & more fun.  
Don’t compare, don’t whinge,  just enjoy or get out of town & find somewhere else.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text:
​@FindingGinaMarie Unfortunately like many visitors to Melbourne you appear to have visited mostly 'tourist traps' that are frequented by visitors rather than locals. South Yarra is probably one of the most 'Sydney like' suburbs in Melbourne so it is rather ironic that you were based there on your trip. Lygon street was a vibrant place for food, coffee and culture in the 90's and early 2000's but has fallen out of favour with locals these days due to high prices and gentrification. 
Victoria Market is a similar situation. People from overseas, interstate and 'out of town' flock to that particular market on their travels to Melbourne though it tends to be a far less frequent shopping destination for Melburnians.
 Street Art is something that the majority of people that live in Melbourne very much take for granted and has been actively encouraged and funded by local councils over the past twenty years to boost tourist numbers in the city. It is often amusing to see people paying money to join organised tours of dirty alleys. 
School groups and tourists are often seen in large numbers at the Shrine of Remembrance and the (lovely) botanical gardens, though unfortunately you would find yourself hard pressed to find a local whom had visited either place less than a handful of times in their entire life.
 It is a great city in many ways and of course it is always interesting to see it from an outsiders perspective.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text:
Sydney is a show-off. Melbourne is substance.         Melbourne is the book. Sydney is the film.          Sydney is emotion. Melbourne is intellect.
The weather in Melbourne is variable but we work with it and enjoy it - especially when there is a heatwave and we know that soon there will come a cool change.
In fact, according to statistics, Sydney has more rain than Melbourne. Sydney had torrential downpours whereas Melbourne has lighter rain over a longer period - which is good for its reputation as 'the Garden City'.
Melbourne is known as the Arts, Cultural, Academic and Sports city of Australia. An international panel has rated Melbourne as 'the world's most liveable city' quite a number of times and it is always in the top ten of those. 
 Melbourne was the richest city in the world in the mid 1800s due to the gold found in its state of Victoria and the resulting goldrush. This is why Melbourne has beautiful heritage buildings and planned street structure and gardens -- the city had money.
There is far more artwork to be seen in the many city lanes which Melbourne kept whereas Sydney demolished their laneways. Melbourne has many arts festivals throughout the year.
Melbourne is known as the coffee cafe culture of Australia. It's not just about the coffee but more ! It's about the love of the relaxed time of sitting down with friends and family with a cup of coffee -- and even talking to strangers nearby. Melbourne is a very friendly city with people of over 200 ethnicities living there.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text:
As a Sydneysider, whilst I appreciate your love of your incredible and awesome city, I’m saddened that you’ve misunderstood Sydney. 
Sydney isn’t just a firework exploding and everyone goes “wow” only to have it fizzle out. 
There is incredible substance to Sydney beyond the initial harbour views. You only need to scratch the surface. 

Like Melburnians tell everyone to spend a little time getting to know the place, the same applies with Sydney. 

It’s sad that tourists here “do the sights” but only get a glimpse of the facade. They literally stand outside the Opera House taking hundreds of selfies but never go inside to take in a show. With 1,800 performances annually it is one of if not the busiest opera houses or theatre complexes in the world.

We have theatres and concert spaces that are ignored by visitors because they’re too busy taking photos of Bondi Beach (which absolutely no Sydneysider would recommend). 

When you are ready, and happy to learn more about Sydney, then listen to your own advice about Melbourne: slow down, ask the locals, find out where the real heart and soul if the city is. 

Get to Cabramatta, or King Street Newtown, or Parramatta, or Balmain, or Surrey Hills, or the Shire, or the Northern Beaches, or Burwood, or Chatswood, or Parklea Markets, or Sydney Markets, or all the other places only locals go and love. Leave the harbour, The Rocks, The Royal Botanic Gardens, and Circular Quay behind, and go to Darling Square, or the largest Chinatown in the Southern hemisphere, or next door Thaitown and Koreatown, or visit the third largest fishmarkets on the planet. 

Do for Sydney what we Sydneysiders now do for Melbourne. Drop the outdated 20th Century competition, and celebrate the amazing diversity and differences of both our unique and distinctive cities. It may just make you celebrate and enjoy this country more.

We had an incredible time there, and if you watched our entire video, we say at the end that they each are unique in their own way and neither city is better than the other. BUT Sydney is a flashier city and you "get" it right away. Melbourne is a slower burn that takes more time to understand. We did a tour of the Opera House. We didn't just stand outside it. We visited Sydney when we still had jobs and less time to explore, and we had a great time. As full-time travelers, we had more time to linger in Melbourne, and so we did. videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text:
We are under the effects of the La Nina ENSO cycle, this results in a lot of rain and storms for the eastern side of Australia and dryer conditions for Western USA, this cycle is less common than El Nino which results in drier conditions here and often drought conditions and more rainfall on the West coast of the USA.

Melbourne averages 163.4 rainfall days with 465mm of rainfall, Sydney is 143.4 days with 1,212.1mm of rainfall.  When it rains in Sydney, it can rain very hard and for a long time where we get shorter showers for the most part.  Things are much worse under a La Nina ENSO cycle.  Sydney has had 9,300mm of rain this year and Melbourne has had 561.4mm, Sydney has been hit with some brutal storms this year.

Global warming has seen Melbourne typically inherit weather like Sydney used to have and Sydney has inherited weather similar to what Brisbane used to have, there are pros and cons with the change of environment.  It used to be miserable outside for much of the year here in Melbourne in the 60s/70s but it is pretty mild and stable here now and we very rarely get hit with freak storms.

We wanted to visit Phillip Island, but we've heard a lot of negativity about how tourists are spoiling it by taking flash and distressing the penguins, and we didn't want to promote something that already gets a lot of attention. videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text: Hi @mooshie79 As full-time travelers, we've learned that we can't see everything because this is our life, not a four-week vacation. We need time to work (Youtube takes a tremendous amount of time) and we still need to do laundry and cook some meals and talk to family members and balance our finances. We have no home after this trip for us to do those things. But we did drive through Dandenong Ranges, but it was raining on the day our friends took us there. The photos were not worth sharing in this video. And while Great Ocean Road is celebrated, since we don't hire vehicles, we didn't have the ability to find a nice day and drive there. We would have had to pay for a tour and cross our fingers that the weather would have been good. It's no fun to share videos of gloomy weather and the outdoor camera we use is a DJI Osmo Pocket 3 that doesn't do well in the rain. We look at it as a reason to return. —Judy

text: Great video I enjoyed watching it. Melbourne is also the sports capital of Australia and one of the major sports capitals of the world. We have the Australian Open tennis which pulls in the largest crowds of any of the tennis opens. And Melbourne also has the F1 Grand Prix which also pulls in more crowds than any other Grand Prix in the world. Plus Melbourne has the second largest cricket Stadium the MCG and Melbourne is home to the Beloved AFL. Plus Melbourne has hosted the Olympic Games and the Commonwealth Games. All of this is quite a great achievement considering Melbourne is relatively small compared to other great cities like London New York or Los Angeles

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text: As unguided neophytes, you hit some of the most noted spots around central Melbourne. If you come back, get someone local to help you find the stuff you can’t see without prior knowledge; also, next time, I would recommend hiring a car for part of the visit, as there’s a lot that wouldn’t be accessible without it.

videoTitle: Why Melbourne Feels Different Than Sydney
channelTitle: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World
text: Hi @gamortie, We had local viewers that tried to show us around, but the weather overall wasn't cooperative for certain places (like the Dandenong Ranges) and some of the places we visited were their recommendations. Our coffee tour showcased a few hidden gems we also might have missed. We're okay with missing out on places that require a vehicle because there actually is a lot to see. As you noticed, there was so much more we could have explored. It's actually not our preference to see everything in a place. While we missed a lot, we experienced a lot just by spending enough time here. Our video wasn't intended to be a Top 10 or Top 20 list, but more of a video about how it felt to stay a month through our eyes. Thanks for watching!  —Judy

text:
I have loved this series but it’s definitely wrong in this case. Sydney-aiders are the ones obsessed with the comparisons. Absolutely obsessed. 
I have half my family line living in both for over 30 years and travel constantly between the two.
I have never heard a Melburnian dunk on the harbour either. It’s absolutely world beating. The Hawkesbury river area is also stunning and underrated. As a Mexican,I’m actually proud of Sydney. To be negative, the people are uptight and the suburbs are cultural vacuums (Melbourne burbs aren’t great either).
A common thing I hear from visitors is this “Put Melbourne city on the harbour and it would be the worlds greatest”
I love how different they are.

text: Why this desperate need to always compare Sydney and Melbourne?!  I've lived in both, I just moved to Melbourne.  Sydney is sunny, has glorious beaches, is exciting and very beautiful with the sea lapping at every corner you turn.. and Melbourne is utterly charming, with the low rise suburbs full of cottages, unchanged for decades, winter landscapes, golden avenues carpeted with autumn leaves, streets of buzzing restaurants full of happy people, coffee shops, people out and about of all ages, markets, a reason to dress up in a beautiful tailored coat..  both cities are fabulous.  Stop with this mean comparison.  And both cities have fabulous coffee!  I'm from London and Bristol in the UK and they are fabulous also.

Sydney can be so busy, and it's so big that you can't know every road (especially as an implant like me). So you're constantly coming up against needing to change lanes unexpectedly. So you're freaur tly appearing "unprepared" and needing to change lanes, and needing people to let you in.
I feel that once you get used to this idea, Sydney drivers seem normal.
I went back to Newcastle and had a few close calls. I think perhaps I used to leave a huge space and wait a long time to be absolutely sure nothing could go wrong. Now I have no time for that.

videoTitle: Starving to pay rent: The brutal reality of the cost of living crisis | 60 Minutes Australia
text:
Lifecreep matters too. I'm WEEEELLLL BELOW 180K.. I'm on 75k. I'm doing fine. I'm even saving!  But if you ask me, 
- what clothes I'm wearing, 2nd hand. Big-W.  
- Car? the most cheapeast reliable
- holiday - maybe once every 5 years?  and no more than 4k
- food? off cuts.
- takeaway? what are those? 
- house? i used to have one. I'm divorced so I'm renting - sharehouse.
- family? don't ask me. I would be different I'm not in that world
- child support? YUP! Check.

I'm not taking away inflation etc.etc. but you also need to factor in what you do with your money. I've downgraded everything. Most people are complaining because, they want a lifestyle. My lifestyle is just above survival - most people are not comfortable here and would say, what's the point. 

I'm not discounting anything here - i'm just sharing - different levels/types of lives people live. I came from a developing country so anything in Australia are huge upgrades. The lifestyle I'm living now - just above survival in Australian terms? It's almost middle class terms from where I came from.

text: This family needs serious budgeting advice.  Even with a rent rising to over $48,000 per year, it is still below 1/3rd of their income.  Without wishing to lay blame, I suspect they have not been completely honest about their debts.  I feel sorry for them, but with a bit of financial help they will get out of the mess they are in.  I really feel sorry for the elderly pensioners and the young trying to make a go of life.  For the young ones, budget, budget, budget and stick to it - don't let the capitalist trap of keeping up with others get into you.  There is very good free financial advice available.  Hang in there.

text: I am on disability, husband my carer on carers. We pay $470 a week rent . That’s half what that couple pay. We are grandparents with 2 kids in their 20s. I am gobbsmacked at their income being $180,000 and they only pay double what we do. I am high needs bedridden. My medication is $300 a month with a lot taken off the pbs even after hitting the threshold. I have a stoma, seizures and need cbd oil without thc which is prescribed but $110 a month, my bladder spasm medication is over $70 a month. None of my bills have anything to do with what’s going on overseas. Food has tripled in price in 3 years. I have 1 meal a day. Not good with 3 autoimmune diseases and a nueurological disease. I have lost so many public specialists. Why? The government again. I worked till serious autoimmune diseases hit me in my mid 30s. How can a couple with 1 child need help on $180,000. I just feel where is our hope. We have no debt but rent are this couple in debt????? Because I just can’t relate to their situation sorry.

videoTitle: Why Are These Melbourne Suburbs So Popular? (Would You Live Here?) | Moving to Australia
text: My Nan & Pa lived on Carlton. My mum grew up in Carlton.  Mum adored her parents. Myself and siblings had to take it in turns to sleep over on weekends and take a friend. I remember catching a bus or walking to the city of Melbourne. My parents used to call their home, Happiness House. Nan played cards in the city,  it  wasn’t for money, it was a social activity, it was on the 6th floor of the Easy Walking building in the city (if my elderly memory is still working lol) Nan would take us and visit her neighbours. I remember her delicious & all her home made meals. Nan was born in 1886 & passed aged 92. Pa was 2 yrs older. So many more  happy memories of Carlton with Nana and Pa, mum’s wonderful Carlton parents.

videoTitle: Why Are These Melbourne Suburbs So Popular? (Would You Live Here?) | Moving to Australia
text:
A well presented video of my home city. Just a few comments especially to respond to those already posted. Melbourne is not always grey, in fact this video was posted on a clear blue winters day it seems. Melbourne does have challenging weather, but it's mild with the exception of the occasional heat wave. No climate is perfect, in fact I live mostly in the Asian tropics and the 4 month long wet season is hideous with its off the scale humidity. Fact : Melbourne has much less annual rainfall than Sydney.
This video was deliberately focused on the livability of CBD and inner suburbs (although sadly the west was committed)
I used to live in Albert Park for 20+ years, so St Kilda was my playground. Currently I live in Fitzroy (on the Carlton border) and wouldn't live anywhere else. South Yarra has never been a favourite, I personally find it bland and homogenised (Richmond is much more interesting)
All these places are blessed with huge parks and public spaces, well served by different modes of public transport.
And yes it is expensive, the price you pay for all these conveniences.
In summary, we all have different preferences and needs. As a retiree of 70, I would move back to Melbourne in a heartbeat.

Why Are These Melbourne Suburbs So Popular? (Would You Live Here?) | Moving to Australia
text: If I could I'd move to Europe in a blink. There is no culture of note in Australia.  There is no cuisine of note in Australia. The people are for the most part unsophisticated, uncivilised and many are extremely rough around the edges. There is increasing levels of crime everywhere and none of the main cities are safe after dark. The summer heat in many parts is absolutely brutal. In the southern states, swimming in the beaches means you are shark bait. Do yourself a huge favour and abandon any thought of moving to Australia. There are so many incredible and infinitely better European locations for you to consider, especially on the Mediterranean.

videoTitle: Why Are These Melbourne Suburbs So Popular? (Would You Live Here?) | Moving to Australia
text: $ price for renting or buying doesn’t just depends on the property size. School zones can be a major factor. Good public (government) schools can save parents tens of thousands of dollars in private school fees. A lot of $smart parents rent or buy in a particular zone for the first four years of a child’s Secondary education and save their money to put their child into a top private school for the final two years to raise their chances of achieving a place at a preferred University. It’s a strategy some are prepared to take but still depends on the student achieving a good VCE score. University places depend on availability and overseas students, especially Asian students, pay a lot of money to attend Australian universities so competition for placement in some subjects is strong. Melbourne has over 280K students attending our 8 Universities spread out over many campuses. There are over 1K suburbs so research will be important if you have higher education expectations for your children. In conjunction with our Universities we also have TAFE colleges that provide vocational training and there are trade schools. I can’t speak to housing around Collingwood because I live in the outer Eastern suburbs. Each suburb will have play grounds, sporting facilities and parks and gardens. Child care and kindergarten placements are something I can’t tell you anything about as I have been the provider of childcare in my family of my grandchildren.  I hope this info helps.

videoTitle: Should you buy property in Melbourne? (KPMG 2026 prediction)
text:
“Be fearful when others are greedy and to be greedy only when others are fearful.”

Melbourne is heavy undervalued and seems like a lot of people are more so stun locked on the “high crime rate” and “political views” rather than genuine statistics and investment mindset. Even with stamp duty and property tax it’s still an incredible opportunity to buy in Melb. Fastest growing city with immigrants which is a good thing! Great food, some of the best night life in the world, Mecca of footy. People often over look that there are actually nice beaches regionally and even close to Melbourne in general. Amazing city. As someone who grew up in Western Australia I think people are really missing out on the opportunities Melbourne will bring into the future. 10 to 20 years now it’s going to be crazy good.

I feel honestly the West is very underrated also. A lot of stigma of it being lower social economic but they said the same about Armadale, maddington,midland and Rockingham to name a few in WA and those places have gone gang busters. Honestly if you had a brain you would snap up properties in the west and just sit on them and or develop and hold for 10 to 15 years.

Investing in real estate is a long game and it takes some foresight to see this is the opportunity of a life time. Also to add the crime is being overblown, heaps of crime in WA and Brisbane all the time and tbh it’s Australia lol the crime rates are low ffs it’s not Afghanistan haha, stop buying into sensationalist media propaganda.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
Sydney had a major train accident in 1977 at Granville,
with considerable loss of life which could have been far higher if a suburban set had been the train derailed or if a couple of trains had been involved.
Priority for rail safety has been on the first rung of Government commitment since that time apart from a short relapse during the Greiner Government when there was another loss of life accident.

Sydney now has all welded track and concrete sleepers, modernised points and signalling, there is always ongoing rail safety improvements under all governments in NSW.
Melbourne has relied on poor quality labour intensive maintenance track with wooden sleepers, connecting plates with the resulting speed restrictions for this type of track. 
Far too much money is thrown at giga road programs that have little capacity, they shortcut on rail safety and ride quality.
They may learn the lesson the hard way NSW did, I don't feel safe on a Melbourne train.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
@kcobley Yeah, there was Glenbrook and Waterfall as well.
I remember as a kid around 1987, I was at a friend's house in Springwood. We were playing in his backyard when we heard a large crash. We went for a walk, half expecting to see a truck accident on the highway. What we didn't expect to see was a V-set train off it's rails, across Macquarie Road and into someone's front yard. I'll never forget it.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
@OldAussieAds Train accidents can be very serious and the potential for loss of life is great, a Syd Train set with 1200 commuters derails at 80 k, the passengers continue to travel at 80 k whist the train stops. 
Obstructions like catenary poles can be deadly in train derailments.
The rolling stock, track, signals have to be meticulously maintained and staffed regardless of cost, technical advances in safety must be quickly advanced into operation. 
There was a huge death toll at the tube accident at Moorgate because advances in safety to a known issue weren't deployed due to cost restraints.
There are still a number of Granville style bridges in operation with spindly metal legs with heavy concrete roadways. 
Goulburn St parking station needs to be pulled down, should never have been built. Some reinforcements of the original legs and speed restrictions imposed.
In the 60's and 70's it always concerned me.
German and Chinese research have concluded trains with engineered permanent coupling are much safer in derailment,
as they tend to stay upright in line and don't zig zag across lines and trackside obstructions.
All new Chinese passenger trains are built permanently coupled or as trains in one unit

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
Coming off from watching both your video and Phillip's video, I do largely agree with both your opinions that Sydney generally has better public transport in Melbourne. 

I feel one of the most important factors to public transport uptake is frequency. As it's said; frequency is freedom, and Sydney has much superior frequencies with most stations having a train at least every 15 minutes for most hours of the day 7 days a week. Melbourne in contrast has most stations with a train every 20 minutes and this often lowers to every 30 minutes on most lines in the evening. Most Melbourne train lines still have trains every 40 minutes until 10am on a Sunday morning which is completely unacceptable for a city of over 4 million people with many people traveling into the city at that time of the morning. 

I also agree with TOD's around stations and the bus network; Sydney not only has more frequent bus services around the city but there's also a higher prevalence of bus priority infrastructure that makes the service faster, more reliable, and more attractive to commuters. Buses in Melbourne pale in comparison to Sydney's and while some small areas have seen recent improvements the majority of the bus network hasn't undergone significant network changes in decades. This leaves a highly inefficient network that is slow and infrequent and doesn't meet the needs of the traveling public.

One area that I feel neither video touched on is that subjectively I believe generally Sydney has better train stations than Melbourne as they have more amenities and shelter compared to Melbourne train stations, are better maintained, and facilitate better transfers between different modes of transport. Granted Melbourne has rebuilt plenty of train stations in recent years which is great to see, but some of these new stations aren't the best designed including poor shelter and transfers between train & bus services (I'm signalling out Mentone for this where they rebuilt the station but didn't bother relocating the bus interchange to the new station). 

One area where I do think Melbourne does better than Sydney is accessibility on the rail network, with all but one station in Melbourne has step-free access. Granted this does not mean that all stations in Melbourne are DDA compliant as there are plenty of older stations that aren't with steep ramps and non-level platforms that can make accessibility tricky. However this is still superior to Sydney where a number of stations do not have step-free access at all. Granted the Transport Access Program has made strides in rectifying this by upgrading stations with lift access but there's still a ways to go, particularly in regional areas. 

Another area where I find Melbourne to be superior to Sydney is its regional rail network with V/Line generally offering a faster and more frequent service from Melbourne to its regional centers compared to NSW Trainlink with Sydney. V/Line offers speeds up to 160km/h to the regional centres with most interurban areas having a train at least once every hour, with Geelong and Ballarat enjoying 20 minute and 40 minute services respectively, plus several daily round-trips to intercity destinations such as Albury, Warrnambool and Swan Hill. 

In contrast NSW Trainlink trains towards Woolongong and Newcastle have only trains once every hour or so and have longer journey times compared to V/Line, although I'll acknowledge that the latter is largely due to Sydney's hilly geography which does slow down trains in contract to Melbourne's relatively flat geography where trains have the ability to run faster.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
In terms of reach, sure. But the scheduling, frequency (operations in general), infrastructure and vessels leave much to be desired. In-line with a tourist water bus network that so happens to be integrated into the local PT system. 

Where on other modes you see investment to either increase frequency, decrease travel time or generally improve the service, Ferries are left to their centurial practices. Often associated with pretty significant taxpayer funding just to maintain it, or degrade it? Which is a shame as Ferries can genuinely be a frequent, reliable, alternate form of public transportation.

(P.S. would love a Building Beautifully episode on Sydney’s Ferries as it’s a mode that’s grossly under appreciated in the PT space.)

text: Why do we need a different card for each public transport system in Australia yet a single E-Tag for every toll road?

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: Because of how the technology works. The etag is just a serial number that stores the real data on a server, whereas transport cards like opal and myki only have a limited amount of memory and even struggle to store an entire district amount of information if every stop needs to be coded, let alone every combination of fare policy. The only way around this is to switch to a server side solution (open loop ticketing), which is what credit-debit card systems do.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: In Japan, they have a unified SUICA, Pasmo and many more IC card systems that just work across most of their JR and Metro systems across the country. Imagine if Australia had a unified ticketing system like OPAL? You wouldn’t need MYKI which absolutely sucks compared to OPAL.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)

Not everyone can obtain a credit card either as they can't be issued to minors. There are places around the world where the one stored value card works for all transport including for domestic air travel purchased directly from the airline and can be tapped on and off on toll roads and parking fees. In addition, the cards will work in most vending machines and for entry to museums and zoos. It isn't rocket science to have a single card that can do everything just a choice that was made by the individual states not wanting to coordinate their systems for the benefit of consumers. It is the modern day equivalent of the states selecting incompatible rail gauges.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
Point 7 cannot be stressed enough - it is key: speed and frequency of express-running medium-distance trains but also what it allows you to do.

Sydney developed quad (4 tracks) all way out into middle and outer suburbs early on (up to St Marys on T1; up to Hurstville on T4; up to Revesby on T8; up to Rhodes plus another section from West Ryde to Epping on T9; and up to Belmore for separated freight on T3), meaning express running on all main lines for outer-suburban, interurban and intercity trains was simple and easy. Whereas Melbourne only developed triple (3 tracks) into the middle suburbs - and even then only on 3 main lines (Frankstone line to Moorabin along with quad track from South Yarra to Caulfield; Lilydale/Belgrave line to Box Hill; Werribee line from Newport to Werribee). Track speeds within Metro Melbourne are limited to 80kmh where Sydney has many lines with decent track sections of up to 100-115kmh.

What this all means is Sydney has regular and (relatively) fast express trains allowing you to get from places 20-30km away from the city like Sutherland, Blacktown, Revesby or Hornsby into the city consistently quicker than equivalent areas in Melbourne, with more flexibility to handle disruptions - and many of these journeys will be shortened further when Sydney Metro opens. What this also allows Sydney to do is split inner and outer sections of lines to run different services: on top of the conversion of the whole T3 to Metro, the inner sections of the T2 T4 T8 and T9 may eventually see a similar conversion to rapid transit with high-capacity high-frequency operations at all inner stops, with suburban inter-urban and intercity services focused purely on the middle and outer suburbs. Melbourne cannot do this without building new lines.

Melbourne's trams really aren't that special in performance terms; of the 7 busiest routes in Melbourne (3, 11, 59, 75, 86, 96 and 109) only Route 96, a former heavy rail line, comes close to the L2 & L3 in Sydney in terms of patronage/km or patronage/stop, and all Melbourne lines have slow average speeds (15-18kmh) even outside the city area with many shared traffic sections causing trams to get stuck in traffic, while not many stops have level boarding & disability access.

Where Melbourne does have an advantage is coverage, there are no major areas of the city as poorly covered by heavy or light rail in Melbourne as in Sydney (Northern Beaches, SE suburbs, outer SW around Bonnyrigg and Wetherill Park) except perhaps Doncaster (which is planned to be linked with Suburban Rail Loop). Another advantage for Melbourne is being able to run its 3 main regional services (Ballarat, Bendigo and Geelong lines) on the one Regional Rail Link direct and at quite high speeds, whereas all Sydney's regional trains split up quite close to the city and have comparatively slow speeds, hence why NSW was doing planning on an entirely new High Speed Rail network to Newcastle, Wollongong, Canberra/Southern Highlands and Bathurst/Orange - which again would be difficult to implement without expensive city access lines to meet at Parramatta or Olympic Park.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
Quad track will be completed on the T1 Northern Line in the next few years between Strathfield and Epping and triplication between Epping and Hornsby as the next stage of the Northern Sydney Freight Corridor program, which is jointly funded by the State and Federal governments.  In the longer term, it is proposed to complete the full quad between Strathfield and Hornsby.  This will allow greater flexibility for all stations suburban services to be separated from semi-express suburban, express intercity, long distance regional and freight services.

Similarly, other long term proposals are to extend the existing quad track on the T1 Western Line from St Marys to Penrith and on the T4 Illawarra Line from Hurstville to Sutherland.  Other major infrastructure upgrades which are worthy of consideration are extending the quad on the T8 East Hills Line from Revesby to Glenfield; quadding the T8 South Line from Glenfield to Macarthur, which includes the existing Southern Sydney Freight Line; and notwithstanding Metro West, sextuplication of the T1 Western Line between Homebush and Granville to allow more suburban and intercity express services from the outer west to run through to Sydney Terminal (Central) on the underutilised Main tracks without the need to interchange to Metro West.  The digital signalling and ATO upgrades, which don't get much publicity, will allow for higher frequencies up to 24 trains/hr per line and faster journey times. 

I think it's unlikely that there will be any further metro conversions of existing suburban lines, although compatible single deck suburban trains could potentially operate at higher frequencies on the inner suburban lines at far less cost and disruption to services compared with a conversion.  This was proposed prior to the separate metro network coming onto the scene.  Proposed future inner suburban cross regional links, fully segregated from the existing suburban network, would be more appropriate as metro lines, including a potential rail link to the Northern Beaches.

The Minns' government cancelled the Regional HSR project as it is too expensive and beyond the State government's resources. The best we could hope for is incremental upgrades to the existing lines with curve easing and limited realignments to allow for higher average speeds and a reduction in journey times.  This could be helped with Federal funding once they realise that a HSR line from Brisbane to Melbourne isn't viable in the Australian context and instead shift their focus to upgrading the existing regional and interstate network to Medium  Speed Rail standard (200km/h).

Frequencies of buses and trains, however, Sydney (or at least outside of the outer suburbs and Northern Beaches) wins by a good margin. Totally agree with you there Sharath. I also appreciate being able to find a seat to sit on even during the busiest times - not something you'd get in Tokyo!

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
As a Melburnian its not even close! Sydney's transport is so much better and seamless. Frequency is the biggest and most important difference. It's so much easier to live car-free in Sydney, especially after 7pm. Whereas in Melbourne you better hope you're on a tram line or on one of the few 10 minute frequency train lines. And don't even bother trying to take a bus. We're all just sitting around hoping everything starts changing after the metro tunnel opens like the government subtly hints at, but who's to say. 
Also way-finding, the city circle/loop, automated train announcements, are just so much more polished in Sydney. Even the fares are more reasonable if you look at short trips and the weekly cap. 

And something no one really mentions but was the most noticeable to me when I visited is through-running is way better!  It's so annoying going from Southern Cross to South Yarra and having to unnecessarily wait at Flinders Street for 3 minutes. Or coming from Richmond/Jolimont to Southern Cross and not knowing even knowing if the train will continue to Southern Cross or not. Doesnt happen with Central.

The one thing Sydney could learn from us is that our regional train and coach system is much better, but thats more of a vic vs nsw thing. 
I really hope we can get the standard of PT Sydney has someday.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
Melbourne rail services are constricted by level crossings, a five minute service would require permanent road closure or a more concerted level crossing removal program.
But wait there's more, a large number of stations have no overhead bridges or underpasses, passengers walk across the tracks at the end of the platform like Blackheath.
Then there is the unfenced track that has both formal and informal track crossings with no gates.
No track duplication on some very busy lines, no express services.
It's combination of level crossings,lack of track duplication and unlimited pedestrian access that inhibits any sort of frequency. 
These serious problems need to be addressed before any ultraloop is contemplated.
Level crossing need to go in less than 10 years.
People won't use a rail service that's infrequent, slow and in a permanent condition of unreliability, like Melbourne.
A more affordable airport link is available there is an old freight line that runs from Sunshine, a surface rail line could be continued relatively cheaply adjacent to airport drive around 7 kilometres a fairly slight uphill run suitable for trains, a very short tunnel to the terminal.
Is there something seriously wrong with the planners, duplication of the Sunshine line is also well overdue.
A second Melbourne City tunnel is also a high priority.  
In the words of Jeff Kennet "trains are an unaffordable luxury, roads are a necessity" when considering a Melbourne airport link a long long time ago.
They are pissing too much money at the road wall.

And absolutely you should be focusing on off-peak frequency increases - it is one of the key barriers to increasing mode shift towards public transport. More frequent smaller trains are a better solution than more infrequent larger trains - Government will not increase frequencies to acceptable off peak levels if they are having to run near empty double decker trains every 5 mins.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: @tobyb6248 Again there is no reason not to use shorter tripple deck trains other than overcrowding, however on my line 4 car trains are common during off peak times, filling up most seats. Its a waste of money to run single deck trains off peak, the same as running triple deck trains off peak empty. It also a bigger waste of money having platform doors on stations which never get crowded. Get real, Melbourne has a manual operated metro system using single deck trains with similar or worse frequency's. Its nothing to do with using triple deck rolling stock or single deck rolling stock.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: @Terry-y2r Melbourne does not have a metro system - it is far more similar to legacy Sydney Trains than a metro, including interlining, signalling and frequency. You are not understanding what I am saying though - it is cheaper (especially if the metro is driverless) to run more frequent off peak services - than you could with a double decker (they are not triple deckers - otherwise there would be three decks on top of each other). Sure, going longer distant double deckers are better for seats (though people generally prefer to stand than be in the dreaded middle seats), but a metro is far more comfortable to stand in than a double decker in peak hour.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: @tobyb6248  Sydney  trains have 3 different deck heights, therefore triple deck. It's obvious. Calling it a double decker is a good example of group stupidity. You also need to look up the definition of a metro. A metro is a high-capacity public transportation system that's usually built in urban areas. It's also known as a subway, tube, or underground therefore  Melbourne has a Metro , the city loop is part of it. You clearly don't travel on triple deck trains, I do and have for over 60 years. Only people traveling one or 2 stops choose to stand or those clogging up the doorways with bikes. The Sydney metro supporter claim of lower cost of automation is questionable, as you need to maintain all the extra platform doors on every station and extra trains compared to using tripe deck trains.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: @tobyb6248 The first metro was built for London. The Metropolitan Railway, opening on 10 January 1863 as the world's first underground passenger railway. It was steam powered. METRO is a short hand term of the company name adopted by Paris and others. Thus the Melbourne underground city circle is a metro as is the Sydney city circle. Any underground passenger railway with stations serving a city is a metro.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text:
As this channel has pointed out, the real problem with Sydney - and Melbourne's - transport network is the paucity of circumferential routes (whether public transport or cars). Both are well equipped with radial lines but these are simply less useful than people think. Getting from suburb to suburb rather than heading to and from the CDB can be a nightmare in both cities but (having lived in both). I reckon Sydney is definitely worse that way, especially out west.

As for "turn up and go", I think moving to that is insoluble while we do not have driverless trains - it is just too expensive to run a near-empty train. If we ever get the signalling automation set up so we can safely go driverless this should be easy to fix.

A lot of the issues in Melbourne with the lack of expresses come down to simple capacity meaning no overtaking. Eventually we can hope the suburban rail loop will fix this but, yeah, this is one area Sydney shits all over Melbourne.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: @tobyb6248 a flaw with Sydney’s network is that the T1, T3, T8 are basically parallel and in an east west direction. A rail line that joins the T4 around Hurstville and runs in a north south direction intersecting the T8, T3 and then joining up to Strathfield at the T1 and T9 would help getting around a lot. For example going from Hurstville to Burwood or Parramatta would become way faster instead of having to change at the city.

videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: As a lifelong Sydneysider, who has paused the video at the "Building Beautifully" title screen, off the bat, I'm going to say that inner city Melbourne public transport is far superior to Sydney's due to the extensive tram network and the free network within the CBD limits (if that's still a thing... I think it is). Outside of the inner city though, I'm not too sure how this will go. I'm not overly familiar with Melbourne's outer city network, but very familiar with Sydney's, which I dare say might have the edge for getting into the CBD, but is currently desperately lacking for radial access... Melbourne, on a completely uneducated guess, may not be that far different... Now, to watch the video and see how my opinions change through the course of this video.... videoTitle: Does Sydney have better public transport than Melbourne? (ft. Philip Mallis)
text: Okay, very interesting. I appreciate Sydney all that much more now! Sadly, my trains are intercity trains and do quite often run hourly, which does impact me, but I guess we would be inadvertently in a TOD area. I don't think it was built with TOD in mind, but everyone in our house has made it clear that living close to the local train station is something that we absolutely want top of priority list in the future. Especially as we gravitate more towards an active transport model of society (slowly, very slowly, but I am still hopeful). One of our cars died over the weekend unexpectedly and a year or two ago, this would have seemed crazy, but we are probably not going to replace it with a car, but a cargo bike instead and use mixed-modal transport / active transport options for most of our transport needs. Anyway, I digress. Very interesting. Thank you for educating me about Melbourne's PT.

videoTitle: How to get from Melbourne Airport to the CBD for CHEAP!! (Less than $5)
text:
As a Melbournian, I have used the 901 bus many times. But do not forget to buy a MYKI public transport card at the airport. Just ask someone who looks official for directions. Unfortunately it is $6 for the card plus add $10 to the card for your bus/train fare. This bus route provides a great tour of metropolitan Melbourne, visiting all main shopping centres and train stations that centre out from the city. 
This service is rarely used by the general public to get to the airport and I have never experienced it being jam packed full. 
For a real adventure, take the 4 hour trip to Frankston, then train to the city. Great trip for people watching!

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
Due to Robert Risson, in the 1960s he beefed up the system and defended the tramways against the the RACV and the media which said trams were “old fashioned”. Governments usually replace the head of whatever department they want to cut with a bean counter before slashing services. With the tramways this was not done and Risson vigoursly defended the tramways until the early ‘70s when the government decided to keep them.
One story in 1965, the tram unions were upset when a tram driver died when he was replacing an overhead pole. Risson to counter this held a news conference where he personally stood on top of a tram and demonstrated the correct procedure to replace a pole.
I saw a newspaper photo of him on top of a tram in suit and hat.
FYI, even now, modern trams with pantographs still have an empty pole mount and pole clipped to the roof just in case. You can see this if you can look out the window of a building above the tramline.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
@jdillon8360 The less-heard explanation I saw on another video is that there are additional operational reasons -- if you're running extra trains at rush hour, then in the morning, incoming trains need to come off the loop into the depot, and in the afternoon trains need to come out of the depot into the loop. I think they could come out of the depot and go either way around the loop, but going the "other" way would add a tonne of conflicting movements over flat junctions, limiting capacity and reducing reliability at the exact time of day when the system is under the most strain.

There's probably less of a difference between peak and off-peak demand these days, and modern practices would be to maintain higher frequencies all day where possible, but I gather it's still a factor.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
@jdillon8360 
The Burnley and Northern loops still reverse direction.

The reason for the reverse of direction is largely historic, but there were two reasons:
1) Most of the trains arriving at Flinders Street in the morning were from the east, then had to reverse into the face of following arrivals to stable in the Jolimont yards.  And the reverse in the afternoon.
2) One of the problems was the capacity of Flinders Street to handle the number of passengers.  If trains arrived at Flinders Street in the morning, then around the loop, or came around the loop to Flinders Street in the afternoon to then run to the ends of the lines, they expected that most passengers would still use Flinders Street, so the problem would not be solved.  So it was basically to force passengers to consider using a different station.

In the afternoon, the only way to get from Parliament to Richmond, without going right around the loop, is to catch a Burnley Loop train.  If the Burnley Loop was altered to run anti-clockwise all day, a lot of passengers would be disadvantaged.  If it was altered to run clockwise all day, there would be no way to get directly from Flagstaff to Southern Cross in the morning.  Things like probably will change eventually, but there are complexities to those decisions that most don't appreciate.

text:
@jdillon8360 The only one that could be reversed to achieve that is the Caulfield loop, and there are no plans for that.  And without me thinking it through, that would probably just throw up other problems.

There are actually long-term plans to reconfigure the loops, so that (I may not have this exactly correct), trains from Glen Waverley will enter the Burnley loop near Richmond, but come out at North Melbourne and continue to Upfield (and beyond), and trains from Upfield will enter the Northern loop at North Melbourne and come out near Richmond to continue to Glen Waverley.  As I said, I might have the specifics wrong, but the end result will be the four loops reconfigured to become two double-track tunnels with all trains entering them coming out on the other side and continuing, rather than going around a complete loop and back to where they came from.

As a Melburnian I am very pleased to see someone who doesn’t live here cover our network very well! On that note, there are a few things:
- Lilydale and Belgrave lines have significant single track (Mooroolbark to Lilydale, and Upper Ferntree Gully to Belgrave respectively). 
- The Suburban Rail Loop will be built in several stages; the first being between Cheltenham and Box Hill. The SRL in its entirety will be completed in 2085. 
- The Metro Tunnel is the first of three planned metro tunnels running through the CBD. The second is to run through the CBD into Fisherman’s Bend and meet with Newport. Personally I think that MM2 is more important than SRL to deliver public transport to Fisherman’s Bend. 
- Reason why Melbourne has so many level crossings is because the terrain is very flat.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
Hi, Reece. Love your channel & watch you on Nebula, but it lacks a chat facility. So, here I am.

As a Melburnian whose memory goes back that far, the City Loop was a solution to a problem that has since mostly gone away, leaving us with an odd legacy.

By the 60s the Melbourne rail system had been largely reduced to a Monday to Friday commuting service for office workers living in the eastern and south-eastern suburbs. The demand from there was much, much larger than it was for the western and northern suburbs. (The population centre of Melbourne was something like 15 kilometres to the south-east of the Central Business District.) At the time there were 10 tracks coming from Richmond station in the east (plus 2 from the north-east that terminated at Princes Bridge). To the west, the viaduct between Flinders Street and Southern Cross (then called Spencer Street) had only 4 tracks (since upgraded to six) due to the proximity of the Yarra River, which also constrained expansion of Flinders Street station itself.

Trains coming from the east and south-east needed to turn around at Flinders Street to set off for another bunch of commuters. Drivers had to walk the length of the train and each platform required the tracks from and to the east to split into incoming / outgoing tracks so trains going in opposite directions wouldn't be trying to use the same track. Trains arrived quicker than they could be turned around. The congestion had to be experienced to be believed. It was common for trains loaded with frustrated office workers to wait half an hour for a platform to become available.

The obvious solution was through-running, but the much lower demand from the west meant the trains would be under-utilised. More importantly, the trains were still needed for commuters in the east. They had to turn around without holding up the trains behind them. Hence the idea of the City Loop was born.

Melbourne has since had enormous growth across the entire western arc of the city. Through running is now a viable practice. The new Metro tunnel is a reflection of this new reality.

Melbourne was sensible to keep their tram network so everything still fits together nicely.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
As a former frequent user of the Hurstbridge line, i think a large reason as to why ridership is comparatively low compared to other lines (other than population density) is that the service near the end of the line is nothing more than very poor - especially late at night. I can’t tell you the amount of times I was stranded at night, waiting sometimes up to two hours, for a train that actually goes all the way through to Hurstbridge. A lot of people I’ve chatted to about using the train around that area say they only use it as a last resort or if they’re planning on drinking in the city: largely because of the poor service and the amount of time it takes compared to taking their car. 
I now live in an area where the service provided is nothing short than world class with ridership to match. It’s made it even more obvious to me that if the government is really keen on increasing ridership in the north east, they need to do more to ensure that taking the train from up that way is actually convenient. And before anyone says anything about the current works being done on the Hurstbridge line, yes, it’ll make it better, but not to point of mass adoption.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
Having lived for a few years in Melbourne I indeed loved the tram and train networks. For some reason, it was never needed for me to travel between suburbs, because there aren't many points of interest in those suburbs. It's not like in Paris where you have La Défense outside the city, two airports, the Saclay plateau with all the universities, Versailles and its castle, Saint Germain en Laye, then Créteil etc... In Melbourne, you got everything of interest in or close to the CBD, and suburbs are just where you sleep. The only issue is probably with Monash University in Clayton : it's so far from the CBD, while if you work/study in Melbourne Uni or RMIT, you're in/bordering the CBD. But it never came up that my friends from Clayton wanted a "ring train line" to another suburb of Melbourne. Moreover, and I think this is important, even the radial transit takes a looooong time. Melbourne is a sprawling city the size of Los Angeles. Visiting my friends in Glen Iris from Carlton would take me an hour by tram. That's about 100 stops. Of course the tram doesn't stop a 100 times, which is another tricky thing in the night, you gotta be careful not to miss your stop. 

An issue that's not addressed here though is how very little offer there is during the night... There are buses and that's it. Train and tram service end WAYYYY too early in the night, usually right around midnight. People need to rely heavily on taxis and uber... And if you go home alone and can't afford it, you're stuck with the night buses and it's a nightmare to find one. This was my only issue with public transport in Australia. But otherwise, it was reliable, practical, fun, and quite safe compared to Paris.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
I think Melbourne and Victoria could have a almost perfect transit network if some things are added alongside exisiting projects
- Exisiting trains bottlenecks like flat junctions, signalling and single tracks are fixed and install high capacity signalling 
- Trains run 10 minutes or better thought the day until the last train
- Every tram route is upgraded similar to route 96 (plus add on traffic priority extensively) and run it at least 10 minutes. Also fill trams gaps and make all trams run low floor
- Extensive bus reforms , bus priority like lanes and lights and ensure Major bus routes run 10 minutes , heavily used locals run 20 minutes and the rest 40 minutes and almost all bus routes must harmonise 
- Create a regional Faster Rail by separating all Metro trains away from Vline and freight services by Standardising all tracks in Victoria (besides Metro Trains) , Quad tracking the Pakenham line, reinstate new dedicated tracks between City to Sunshine ,expand the scope of the Airport rail link (remove it from the Metro Tunnel) to connect Bendigo and Northern eastern lines  and electrify all regional rails and possibly create a dedicated Metropolitan express train
- Reopen some closed regional lines and make a standard for all regional services (trains or coaches) to run at least 5 services every day and harmonise with trains through a Swiss Style timetable through Victoria 
- Remove every level crossings in Melbourne including pedestrians
- Build Metro 2
- Extend Metro trains to Doncaster, Rowville, Wollert, Clyde, Baxter, Melton and Wyndham Vale 
- Consider Building a completely new light Metro in the long term. My proposal are 
1) Ringwood to Frankston
2) Rowville to Airport via Ormond, Fisherman's Bend, and Highpoint
3)  Broadmeadows to Monash via Chadstone, the Alamein Line, Richmond, Bourke St, Fishermans Bend and finally utilizing the Newport-Broadmeadows freight line 
4)  Sunshine to La Trobe Uni via Highpoint, Brunswick, and Northland
- New BRT and LRT (other from the exisiting tram Networks)
- East Coast High Speed Rail
- Metropolitan style trains (at a smaller scale) for Geelong, Latrobe Valley, Bendigo and Ballarat
- Bike Lanes and walking paths everywhere and connected
