Another great video. I’d like to see a video on how rail project delivery authorities could improve their PR. They tend to quietly beaver away but fail dismally at keeping the public informed, engaged and importantly enthused for the transformative possibilities these project have for public transit. Good PR is particularly important as these projects cost a lot of money so it’s easy for detractors and vested interests in private transport to shift public opinion into believing the project is a waste of money.
I’m in Brisbane where the government is building Cross River Rail and the delivery authority’s PR is anaemic at best. People don’t realise that this will allow new planned lines, extensions, conversions and reactivation/rebuilding of abandoned/mothballed lines to proceed.

text: @biosparkles9442 yeah and the different colors for zones is ridic bad..

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
​@George2647g Not arguing there, but still isn't a map that's entirely yellow.

What I imagine TD B is remembering are the maps that showed all zone 1 sections in yellow and all zone 2 sections in blue. Since the vast majority of tram lines existed only in zone 1, it resulted in an *almost* entirely yellow tram map, with only the 109, 86, and 75 having sections that were shown in both blue and yellow (as you only got the zone 2 fare if you only travelled in the blue section on trams. Leaving zone 2 on a tram meant zone 1 fare applied to your entire trip). The train map was never entirely yellow, with every line except for Williamstown having sections in zone 2 until the zones were removed entirely in 2015 (a move I did not, and still do not agree with). 

Showing the different fare types visually on the map was useful imo, but it should've been done using a different method than colour coding, as the entire map is much easier to understand when the lines are different colours.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text: @biosparkles9442 Yes I was talking about the map. Zone 1 was yellow, zone 2 was blue, and zone 3 was red. It made using the system much more confusing that it had to be.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
Maybe I'm the weird one, because I preferred the zone based colouration. The system is almost purely radial, and with that knowledge alone, you know where the train is going from and to when you look at the old map (or the new one).
The newer map, with its colour coding based on rail groups, gives information that is of only limited use to the end user. That Glen Waverley and Belgrave are serviced by the same trains, for example, tells me nearly nothing of use.
But having colour tell me how much the journey will cost, is useful.

It did become less useful when zone 3 was abolished and when all of zone 1 became part of the zone 1+2 crossover area, but with the new map I can't even remember where the boundaries between zone 1 and 2 are.

And my preference only applies to the train network. The tram network is definitely best as a route map, as it is almost entirely non-radial.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text: Hello all, the Baxter Electrification Extension is not, at this time (June 2023), going ahead unfortunately.There was a $aud2M Business Study that ultimately demonstrated that it was uneconomic. However, for $2m they could have resleepered the line from Frankston to Baxter ! In my opinion there are a number of factors that probably doomed the idea, namely the cost of meeting the "current standard" which is no level crossings, a replacement signalling system, and the assumption that a Stabling Siding was required at Baxter ( and for which railway owned land has been reserved ), and the assumption that the project would be stand alone, as opposed to a component of a larger Project. As planning approvals to increase housing in Langwarren, Baxter and Sommerville  areas were announced quite some time ago, the Extension of Electrification should be at least to Sommerville, which has sufficient space for a island platform, and a limited ( 2 or 3 track ) stabling sidings if required ( ie, Hurtbridge).Given a frequency of every third train being extended from Frankston, which would be a half hourly service frequency, I would argue that such a service would be well patronised. regards to all.

videoTitle: The Comprehensive "Metro" Network of Melbourne
text:
The weakness of Melbourne's system that doesn't come out in this video is how spread out it is, which means that at the outer extremities where the population growth is, you are rarely very close to a station. As these locations are beyond the spread of the tram network and bus services are poorly planned and infrequent, they become heavily car-dependent. And Mellbourne is still very city-centred compared to Toronto and Sydney, hence the radial network.

Infrastructure like the Suburban Rail Loop (which is far from an outer-suburb service) will need a big improvement to bus services to be useful because its stations will be few and far between. We are going to need improvements such as extending the Cranbourne line to Clyde, electrifying the lines to Melton and Tarneit, and the Geelong fast rail to free up capacity on the western suburbs. Improvement to existing services in strategic areas could give better bang for buck than the high-profile vote-winning expansion projects.

videoTitle: SYDNEY TRAINS VS MELBOURNE TRAINS
text: I've been on trains that needed a button to open the doors, it's a scary thought to think that anyone could open the train doors just by pushing a button. While I have no doubt that some of these types of trains would have safety measures in place to ensure that the doors remain closed while the train is still moving. But a friend of mine who lives overseas had a particularly scary experience once, a train that she was on is similar to the Melbourne trains, you have to push a button on the door for the doors to open, a lunatic managed to open the train doors while it was still moving & they had to make an emergency stop, when passengers have the ability to open the train doors with just a push of a button, it's scary and risky. but on Sydney trains, only the train drivers have control of opening & closing the doors, the doors automatically opens & closes via controls in the driver cockpit, the passengers cannot open the doors, so that eliminates any risks of a passenger trying to open the doors while the train is in transit. It just makes Sydney trains more safer.
